subject.name: System Design
subject.description: Scale from zero to millions of users. APIs, databases, caching, load balancing, and classic design problems — all in interviewer voice.

chapters.title: A Framework for System Design Interviews
chapters.subtitle: Every interviewer expects a structured approach. Without a framework, you ramble. With one, you lead the conversation. This is the 4-step process that works every time.

questions.question: Before we start — what do you think I'm actually evaluating you on in this interview?
questions.tldr: Jumping straight to a solution without understanding the problem. The interview is about the process, not the final diagram.
questions.answer: The interview is not about producing a perfect architecture. It's about showing how you think through ambiguity, make trade-offs, and communicate clearly. Interviewers evaluate your process, not your answer.
points: No "right" answer — Two candidates can propose completely different architectures and both pass, if their reasoning is sound.
Silence kills — If you're thinking, say it out loud. The interviewer can't grade what's in your head.
Don't over-engineer — Designing for 1 billion users when the prompt says "a startup" shows poor judgment.
Don't under-communicate — State your assumptions. Ask clarifying questions. Summarize before moving on.

questions.question: OK, I'm going to give you a design problem. How would you structure the next 45 minutes?
questions.tldr: Step 1: Clarify requirements (3-5 min). Step 2: Estimate scale (3-5 min). Step 3: High-level design (10-15 min). Step 4: Deep dive (10-15 min).
questions.answer: This framework works for every system design question. The time splits may vary, but the order never changes.
questions.diagram:
graph LR S1["Step 1
Requirements
3-5 min"] --> S2["Step 2
Estimation
3-5 min"] S2 --> S3["Step 3
High-Level Design
10-15 min"] S3 --> S4["Step 4
Deep Dive
10-15 min"]
questions.diagramCaption: The 4-step framework — always in this order
points: Step 1 — Requirements — Ask: Who are the users? What are the core features? What does NOT need to be built? What are the non-functional requirements (latency, availability, consistency)?
Step 2 — Estimation — Back-of-the-envelope math: DAU (Daily Active Users), QPS (Queries Per Second), storage, bandwidth. This determines whether you need 1 server or 1000.
Step 3 — High-Level Design — Draw the big boxes: client, API, services, database, cache. Define the API contract. Show the data flow.
Step 4 — Deep Dive — The interviewer picks a component to stress-test. Be ready to go 2-3 levels deep on any box you drew.
questions.followup:
"What if the interviewer interrupts your flow?"

That's the point. The interviewer wants to steer you toward the interesting problems. Don't resist — adapt. If they want to deep-dive early, go with it. You can always say "Let me quickly sketch the high-level first so we have context, then we'll dive in."

questions.question: Design a URL shortener. Go ahead — what's the first thing you do?
questions.tldr: Ask about users, core features, scale, and constraints. Narrow the scope — you have 45 minutes, not 45 days.
questions.answer: Never start designing until you know what you're building. Spend 3-5 minutes asking the right questions. The interviewer wants you to ask — it shows maturity.
points: Functional requirements — "What are the core features?" For a URL shortener: create short URL, redirect, analytics? Or just the first two?
Users and scale — "How many DAU (Daily Active Users)?" "Read-heavy or write-heavy?" "What latency is acceptable?"
Non-functional requirements — Availability vs consistency? What's the SLA (Service Level Agreement)? Do we need exactly-once delivery?
Scope boundaries — "Should I design the auth system too, or assume it exists?" "Mobile and web, or just web?"
Write it down — List requirements on the whiteboard. Refer back to them when making trade-offs.

rows: 1 app server + 1 DB is enough. No sharding needed.
Need load balancers, multiple app servers, DB sharding, CDN, and caching.
Add read replicas + Redis cache in front of DB. Cache hit rate will handle 90% of traffic.
Focus on write throughput — consider write-ahead logs, async writes, or write-optimized DBs like Cassandra.
Must serve from cache or edge/CDN. DB lookup on every request is too slow.
Direct DB reads are acceptable. Simpler architecture, no cache layer needed.
Use eventual consistency, multi-region replication, and DNS failover. Accept stale reads briefly.
Use a single-leader DB with synchronous replication. Accept higher latency and reduced availability during partitions.
Single-region, basic failover. One standby replica is enough.
Multi-region active-active, automated failover, health checks, no single point of failure. Significantly more complex.
What features are out of scope?
Skip user management and event tracking — saves 15 min to focus on core shortening + redirect flow.

questions.question: Hold on — you said "functional" and "non-functional" requirements. What's the difference and why should I care?
questions.tldr: Functional = WHAT the system does (features). Non-functional = HOW WELL the system does it (performance, reliability, scale).
questions.answer: Every system design interview requires you to separate these two. Functional requirements drive your API and data model. Non-functional requirements drive your architecture decisions — caching, replication, sharding, CDN. Miss either category and your design has blind spots.
questions.diagram:
graph LR FR["Functional
WHAT it does"] --> API["Drives API +
Data Model"] NFR["Non-Functional
HOW WELL"] --> ARCH["Drives Architecture
Decisions"]
questions.diagramCaption: Functional requirements shape your API; non-functional shape your infrastructure

rows: Features the system must provide — what users can do
Create short URL, redirect to original, view click analytics
System uptime — can users always reach it?
99.99% uptime (4 nines), no single point of failure
Handling growth in users or data
Does every user see the same data?
Short URL must resolve immediately after creation
Once created, a URL must never be lost — replicated storage

points: Functional requirements come from asking "what" — What can users do? What data is stored? What does the API return? These decide your endpoints and schema.
Non-functional requirements come from asking "how much" and "what if" — How many users? What if a server goes down? What latency is acceptable? These decide your architecture.

SLA (Service Level Agreement) — A contract that defines how reliable the system must be. Measured in "nines": 99.9% (3 nines) = 8.7 hours downtime/year. 99.99% (4 nines) = 52 minutes/year. More nines = exponentially harder and more expensive. Always ask what the SLA target is — it determines whether you need basic failover or full multi-region active-active.
The interviewer tests both — A candidate who only lists features but ignores scale, latency, and failure modes will design a system that works on a laptop but crashes in production.
State them explicitly — Write "FR:" and "NFR:" on the whiteboard. It shows structure and makes trade-off discussions easy later.
questions.followup:
"Which non-functional requirement matters most for this system?"

It depends on the system. For a URL shortener, availability and latency matter most — users expect instant redirects 24/7. For a banking system, consistency and durability dominate — you can't lose a transaction. Knowing which NFR to prioritize is what separates senior from junior candidates.

questions.question: Good, you've got the requirements. Now draw me a high-level design — what goes on the whiteboard first?
questions.tldr: Start with the API, then draw the data flow from client to storage. Add components only when you can justify why they exist.
questions.answer: Don't draw random boxes. Start from the user's action and trace it through the system.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  Client --> API[API Gateway]
  API --> Svc[Service]
  Svc --> Cache[(Cache)]
  Svc --> DB[(Database)]
questions.diagramCaption: The minimal starting point — client, API, service, storage
points: Start with the API — Define 2-3 core endpoints. POST /urls for creating, GET /:shortId for redirecting. This grounds the design.
Trace the data flow — "User sends request → API gateway → service → database. Response flows back."
Add components with justification — Don't add a cache "just because." Say "reads are 10:1 vs writes, so a cache in front of the DB will reduce load by 90%."
Keep it simple first — Start with one server, one database. Then add complexity in Step 4 when the interviewer asks "what if traffic is 100x?"
questions.followup:
"Should I define the database schema at this stage?"

Briefly. Sketch the key tables and their relationships (1-2 minutes). Don't write full SQL — just "Users table (id, name, email), URLs table (short_id, original_url, user_id, created_at)." This shows you think about data modeling.

questions.question: I like your high-level design. Now let me push on a few things — how do you handle that?
questions.tldr: The interviewer picks a component to stress-test. Go 2-3 levels deep. Show you can handle edge cases, failures, and scale.
questions.answer: This is where the interview is won or lost. The interviewer will pick the hardest part of your design and drill into it. Common deep-dive topics:
points: "How does this scale to 10x?" — Be ready to add caching, sharding, or read replicas to any component.
"What happens when X fails?" — Discuss failover, retries, circuit breakers, and data durability.
"How do you ensure consistency here?" — Know the trade-offs: strong vs eventual, sync vs async replication.
"Walk me through this specific request end-to-end." — Trace a single request from client to database and back, naming every hop.
"How would you monitor this?" — Mention metrics (QPS, p99 latency, error rate), logging, and alerting.

questions.question: I've interviewed hundreds of candidates. What do you think makes me immediately lose confidence in someone?
questions.tldr: Jumping to solutions, ignoring scale, not discussing trade-offs, silence, and over-engineering.
questions.answer: Avoid these and you're already ahead of 80% of candidates:

rows: Spend 3-5 min asking clarifying questions first
Drawing boxes with no data flow
Trace a request end-to-end through the system
State the problem, then the solution, then the trade-off
Think out loud — "I'm considering X because..."
Designing for Google scale when the prompt is a startup
Match complexity to the stated scale
Proactively say "if this fails, here's the fallback"
Be ready to deep-dive into any box you drew

questions.question: We have 45 minutes. You just spent 20 on requirements. How do you make sure you don't run out of time?
questions.tldr: Requirements: 5 min. Estimation: 5 min. High-level design: 15 min. Deep dive: 15 min. Wrap-up: 5 min.
questions.answer: Time management is itself a signal. If you spend 20 minutes on requirements and never get to the design, that's a fail. If you rush to deep-dive without a high-level sketch, you'll get lost.
questions.diagram:
graph LR R["Requirements
5 min"] --> E["Estimation
5 min"] E --> H["High-Level
15 min"] H --> D["Deep Dive
15 min"] D --> W["Wrap-Up
5 min"]
questions.diagramCaption: Time allocation for a 45-minute interview
points: Check in with the interviewer — "I've outlined the requirements. Shall I move to estimation, or did you want to adjust scope?"
Don't get stuck — If you're spinning on one component for more than 3 minutes, state your assumption and move on.
Leave time for wrap-up — Summarize what you built, what trade-offs you made, and what you'd improve with more time.

takeaways: 4-step framework — Requirements → Estimation → High-Level Design → Deep Dive. Always in this order.
FR vs NFR — Functional Requirements (WHAT: features, API) vs Non-Functional Requirements (HOW WELL: latency, availability, scale, consistency, durability).
DAU = Daily Active Users — The starting point for all scale estimation. QPS = Queries Per Second = DAU x actions / 86,400.
SLA = Service Level Agreement — 99.9% = 8.7h downtime/year. 99.99% = 52 min/year. More nines = multi-region, no single point of failure.
Ask before designing — 3-5 minutes of clarification prevents 15 minutes of building the wrong thing.
Think out loud — The interviewer grades your reasoning, not your diagram.
Justify every component — Don't add a cache "just because." State the problem it solves.
Trade-offs are the answer — "It depends" is fine, as long as you say what it depends on.
Time management is a signal — 5/5/15/15/5 is the target split.

chapters.title: Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation
chapters.subtitle: Interviewers don't expect exact math. They want to see if you can reason about scale — whether a problem needs 1 server or 10,000. These are the numbers and techniques you need.

questions.question: Before you start drawing boxes, can you estimate the scale of this system for me?
questions.tldr: They're testing if you can reason about scale before you design. Wrong estimates lead to wrong architectures.
questions.answer: If you estimate 100 QPS and it's actually 100,000 QPS, you'll design a single-server system that collapses on day one. Estimation isn't about precision — it's about getting the order of magnitude right so your design matches reality.
points: Order of magnitude — Being off by 2x is fine. Being off by 1000x means your architecture is fundamentally wrong.
Round aggressively — Use 10M instead of 8.7M. Use 100K instead of 86,400. Interviewers prefer clarity over fake precision.
State assumptions — "I'll assume 10M DAU and a 10:1 read-to-write ratio." The interviewer will correct you if you're wrong.
Units matter — Always track units: requests/sec, bytes/day, GB/year. Mixing them up is a red flag.

questions.question: How big is a KB? A GB? A TB? Give me the rough numbers you work with.
questions.tldr: Memorize: 1 KB = 10^3 bytes, 1 MB = 10^6, 1 GB = 10^9, 1 TB = 10^12. These anchor all your estimates.
questions.answer: You don't need to know every power of 2. You need to know the ones that come up in estimation:
points: ASCII char — 1 byte
UTF-8 char — 1-4 bytes (assume 2 for mixed text)
UUID / int64 — 8 bytes
A tweet (280 chars) — ~1 KB with metadata
A photo (compressed) — ~200 KB – 2 MB
A 1-minute video (720p) — ~10 MB

questions.question: How long does a memory read take versus a disk seek? What about a cross-continent roundtrip?
questions.tldr: Memory: ~100ns. SSD: ~100μs. Network (same DC): ~500μs. Network (cross-continent): ~150ms. These drive every caching and replication decision.
questions.answer: These numbers, originally compiled by Jeff Dean, tell you where bottlenecks live. Memorize the order of magnitude, not the exact value:
points: Memory is 1000x faster than SSD — This is why Redis (in-memory) crushes disk-based reads.
SSD is 40x faster than HDD — This is why SSDs are standard for databases.
Same-DC roundtrip is 300x faster than cross-continent — This is why CDNs and multi-DC matter.
Disk seek is the killer — Sequential reads are 100x faster than random reads. This is why LSM trees (used by Cassandra, LevelDB) batch writes.
questions.followup:
"How do these numbers change your design?"

If your p99 latency target is 100ms and a cross-continent roundtrip is 150ms, you must serve from a local DC or edge cache. If your DB read is 5ms and you need 0.5ms, you must add an in-memory cache. The latency numbers turn vague requirements into concrete architecture decisions.

questions.question: Say we have 10 million daily active users. How many requests per second is that?
questions.tldr: QPS = DAU × actions-per-user / 86,400. Peak QPS is ~2-3x average. This tells you how many servers you need.
questions.answer: The formula is simple, but the assumptions matter:
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  DAU["DAU: 10M"] --> Actions["x 5 actions/user"]
  Actions --> Daily["50M requests/day"]
  Daily --> QPS["/ 86,400 = 600 QPS"]
  QPS --> Peak["x 3 = 1,800 peak"]
questions.diagramCaption: QPS estimation flow — from DAU to peak requests per second
points: DAU to QPS — 10M DAU × 5 actions/user = 50M daily requests. 50M / 86,400 ≈ 600 QPS average.
Peak multiplier — Peak is typically 2-3x average. So ~1,800 QPS at peak.
Read vs write — If 90% reads and 10% writes: 1,620 read QPS, 180 write QPS. This determines if you need read replicas.
A single server handles ~1,000-10,000 QPS — Depends on the workload (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound). So 1,800 QPS ≈ 1-2 servers.

questions.question: We're storing a million new records a day. How much disk do we need over 5 years?
questions.tldr: Storage = daily-new-records × record-size × retention-period. Always estimate for 3-5 years, not just day one.
questions.answer: Storage estimation tells you whether you need a single database or distributed storage. The key is estimating record size correctly.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  R["1M records/day"] --> S["x 1 KB each"]
  S --> D["= 1 GB/day"]
  D --> Y["x 1,825 days = 1.8 TB"]
questions.diagramCaption: Storage estimation — from daily records to multi-year capacity
points: Example: URL shortener — 1M new URLs/day × 1 KB/record = 1 GB/day. Over 5 years: ~1.8 TB. Easily fits on a single database.
Example: Instagram — 500K new photos/day × 500 KB average = 250 GB/day. Over 5 years: ~450 TB. You need distributed storage (S3).
Don't forget metadata — For every photo stored, you also store user info, timestamps, tags, likes. That's an extra 1-5 KB per photo.
Replication multiplier — If you keep 3 replicas, multiply total by 3. That 1.8 TB becomes 5.4 TB.

questions.question: Our API serves 1,600 reads per second with 500 KB responses. Is network a bottleneck?
questions.tldr: Bandwidth = QPS × average-response-size. This tells you if your network is the bottleneck.
questions.answer: Bandwidth is often overlooked but critical for media-heavy systems like YouTube or Instagram.
points: Incoming (ingress) — Write QPS × request size. For a URL shortener: 180 writes/sec × 1 KB = 180 KB/s. Negligible.
Outgoing (egress) — Read QPS × response size. For image serving: 1,620 reads/sec × 500 KB = 810 MB/s. This is massive — you need a CDN.
Rule of thumb — If egress exceeds 100 MB/s from a single server, you need a CDN or load-balanced cluster.
Video is the outlier — A 720p video stream is ~5 Mbps per user. 10K concurrent viewers = 50 Gbps. This is why YouTube has edge caches everywhere.

questions.question: Let's say I ask you to design Twitter. Walk me through the numbers before you touch the whiteboard.
questions.tldr: Start with DAU, derive QPS, storage, and bandwidth. Then use the numbers to justify architecture decisions.
questions.answer: This is the structure interviewers expect. State assumptions, do the math, then connect numbers to design decisions.
points: Assumptions — 300M MAU, 50% DAU = 150M. Each user: 2 tweets/day, 10 timeline views/day.
Tweet QPS — 150M × 2 / 86,400 ≈ 3,500 write QPS. Peak: ~10K write QPS.
Timeline QPS — 150M × 10 / 86,400 ≈ 17,000 read QPS. Peak: ~50K read QPS. Read-heavy (5:1 ratio).
Storage — 300M tweets/day × 1 KB = 300 GB/day. With media: assume 10% have images (200 KB avg) → 300M × 0.1 × 200 KB = 6 TB/day in images alone.
Bandwidth — Timeline reads: 50K QPS × 10 KB (20 tweets × 500 bytes) = 500 MB/s. Add images and it's 10x more → CDN is mandatory.
Key decisions from numbers — Write QPS of 10K? A single DB can't handle that → need sharding. Read:write ratio of 5:1 → heavy caching. 6 TB/day in images → S3 + CDN, not your own servers.
questions.followup:
"How accurate do these numbers need to be?"

Within an order of magnitude. If you say 3,500 QPS and reality is 5,000, your design is still correct. If you say 35 QPS, you'll design a single-server system that crashes at launch. The point is to land in the right ballpark — not to be a calculator.

questions.question: You said 600 QPS but your system just crashed at peak. Where did your estimation go wrong?
questions.tldr: Forgetting peak traffic, ignoring storage growth over time, mixing up units, and being too precise with round numbers.
questions.answer: These mistakes seem minor but they undermine your credibility:

rows: Project 3-5 years for storage and growth
1 MB = 8 Mb. Network speeds are in bits, storage in bytes
Every record has overhead: indexes, timestamps, pointers

questions.question: OK, you've got 50K QPS at peak. How many servers do you actually need to handle that?
questions.tldr: Servers needed = Peak QPS / QPS-per-server. A typical web server handles 1K-10K QPS depending on workload complexity.
questions.answer: This is the final connection between estimation and architecture. After you know your peak QPS, you can estimate infrastructure:
points: Simple API server — ~5,000-10,000 QPS per instance (in-memory operations, small payloads).
Database-heavy server — ~1,000-2,000 QPS per instance (each request hits DB).
Media processing — ~50-200 QPS per instance (CPU-intensive: image resize, video transcode).
Example — Peak 50K read QPS for timeline. With cache hit rate of 90%, only 5K QPS hit the DB. At 2K QPS per DB instance, you need ~3 read replicas.
Add headroom — Plan for 2x current peak. If you need 3 replicas now, provision 5-6 to handle growth and failures.
questions.followup:
"How do you know the QPS capacity of a single server?"

Load testing. In interviews, use rules of thumb: a simple Node.js/Go API handling ~5K QPS, a Java/Spring app handling ~2K QPS with DB calls. In production, you benchmark your code on your hardware. Never guess — measure.

takeaways: Order of magnitude — Being off by 2x is fine. Being off by 1000x means the wrong architecture.
QPS formula — DAU × actions / 86,400. Peak = 2-3x average.
Storage formula — Daily records × size × retention years.
Memorize latency gaps — RAM (100ns) vs SSD (100μs) vs HDD (4ms) vs cross-DC (150ms). These drive every cache/replication decision.
Always estimate peak — Average QPS is meaningless for capacity planning.
Connect numbers to design — "50K QPS → need caching. 6 TB/day → need S3 + CDN." That's what interviewers want to hear.

chapters.title: Core Concepts Every Interviewer Expects You to Know
chapters.subtitle: Before you design anything, you need a shared vocabulary with the interviewer. These are the building-block concepts that come up in every single system design discussion. Get these wrong and nothing else matters.

questions.question: I say "stateless" and "stateful" a lot in these interviews. What do those mean to you?
questions.tldr: Stateless = server stores nothing between requests; every request is self-contained. Stateful = server remembers previous interactions.
questions.answer: This distinction drives how you scale. Stateless servers can be swapped, replaced, or multiplied freely because they don't hold user data. Stateful servers are tied to specific sessions — you can't just kill one without losing data.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  C1[Client A] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  C2[Client B] --> LB
  LB --> S1[Server 1]
  LB --> S2[Server 2]
  LB --> S3[Server 3]
  S1 --> DB[(Shared Store)]
  S2 --> DB
  S3 --> DB
questions.diagramCaption: Stateless: any server can handle any request because state lives in a shared store

rows: Need sticky sessions or state migration
REST API server behind a load balancer
Game servers, in-memory cache on a single node

points: Stateless is the default goal — In 90% of system design answers, you want stateless application servers. State goes into a database or Redis, not server memory.
Stateful is not always bad — WebSocket connections, in-memory caches, and game servers are intentionally stateful. The trade-off is harder scaling and complex failover.

Interview signal — When you draw an architecture, the interviewer will ask "what happens if this server dies?" If the answer is "nothing, because state is external," you just proved you understand stateless design.
questions.followup:
"Your WebSocket server is stateful and holding 100K connections. How do you scale it?"

Use a pub/sub layer like Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka. Each server manages its own connections, but messages are broadcast through the shared channel. If a server dies, clients reconnect to another server and resubscribe. You accept a brief disconnect — the trade-off for horizontal scaling.

questions.question: I need two services to communicate. When would you use synchronous vs asynchronous communication?
questions.tldr: Sync (HTTP/gRPC) = caller waits for a response. Async (queues/events) = caller fires and moves on. Sync is simpler; async is more resilient at scale.
questions.answer: This is one of the most frequent trade-off questions in interviews. The choice affects latency, coupling, failure handling, and scalability.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  subgraph Synchronous
    A1[Service A] -- "HTTP call, waits" --> B1[Service B]
    B1 -- "response" --> A1
  end
questions.diagramCaption: Sync: Service A blocks until Service B responds
questions.diagram2:
graph LR
  subgraph Asynchronous
    A2[Service A] -- "publish message" --> Q[(Queue)]
    Q -- "consume" --> B2[Service B]
  end
questions.diagram2Caption: Async: Service A publishes and moves on; Service B processes when ready

rows: Caller sends request, blocks until response
Caller puts message on queue, continues immediately
Slower end-to-end, but caller is not blocked
Tight — if B is down, A fails
Loose — if B is down, messages wait in queue
Queue retries automatically; dead-letter queue for failures
User needs an immediate answer (login, search)
Work can be deferred (send email, process image, analytics)
Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS, Redis Streams

points: <mark>Default to sync for user-facing requests</mark> — When a user clicks "submit," they expect a response. Use HTTP or gRPC.
<mark>Default to async for background work</mark> — Sending emails, resizing images, updating analytics — the user does not need to wait for these.
Async absorbs traffic spikes — A queue acts as a buffer. If 10K messages arrive in 1 second, workers process them at their own pace. Without a queue, your service falls over.
The trade-off — Sync is simpler to debug and reason about. Async introduces eventual consistency, message ordering challenges, and infrastructure complexity (queue management, DLQs).
questions.followup:
"Your order service calls the payment service synchronously. Payment is slow. What do you do?"

Two options: (1) Keep sync but add a timeout + circuit breaker so you fail fast instead of hanging. (2) Switch to async — accept the order immediately, put a payment job on a queue, and notify the user when payment completes. Option 2 is better at scale but requires handling the "payment failed after order was accepted" edge case.

How to handle the edge case: Create the order in a "pending" state — not confirmed. The payment worker processes the job. On success, update order to "confirmed" and notify the user. On failure, update order to "payment_failed," release any reserved inventory, and notify the user to retry. Never mark an order as confirmed until payment succeeds. This is called a saga pattern — a sequence of local transactions where each step has a compensating action if a later step fails.

questions.question: You keep hearing "vertical scaling" and "horizontal scaling." Give me the one-liner and tell me when each makes sense.
questions.tldr: Vertical = bigger machine. Horizontal = more machines. Start vertical, go horizontal when you hit limits.
questions.answer: Every scaling discussion in an interview starts here. Know the boundary where one stops working and the other takes over.
questions.diagram:
graph LR V["Vertical
Bigger Machine"] -.- VL["Limit: single
machine max"] H["Horizontal
More Machines"] -.- HL["Limit: complexity
of distribution"]
questions.diagramCaption: Both strategies have ceilings — know when to switch

rows: Upgrade CPU, RAM, SSD on one machine
Add more machines behind a load balancer
Zero — your code does not change
High — need load balancing, state management, data partitioning
Cheap at first, then exponentially expensive
One server dies, others keep serving

points: <mark>Start vertical, go horizontal</mark> — A startup with 1K users does not need Kubernetes. A bigger database instance is faster, cheaper, and simpler.

Databases resist horizontal scaling — Sharding a relational DB is complex (cross-shard joins, transactions). This is why many companies scale vertically on the DB as long as possible, then add read replicas before sharding. Real examples: StackOverflow serves 1.3B+ page views/month on a single SQL Server instance (vertical). Instagram sharded PostgreSQL only after hitting 25+ TB. Shopify ran on a single MySQL leader for years before sharding by shop_id.
App servers love horizontal scaling — If they are stateless, you just add more behind a load balancer. Auto-scaling handles traffic spikes.
Interview tip — Always start your design simple (1 server). Then when the interviewer says "now handle 10x traffic," scale horizontally. This shows you think incrementally.

questions.question: What does "latency" vs "throughput" mean? And why can optimizing one hurt the other?
questions.tldr: Latency = how fast one request completes. Throughput = how many requests the system handles per second. Batching improves throughput but hurts latency.
questions.answer: These two metrics are the foundation of all performance conversations in interviews. They are related but not the same — and sometimes they fight each other.

rows: Time for ONE request: start to finish
Total requests processed per unit of time
Page load speed, API response time
System capacity — can it handle the traffic?
Caching, CDN, faster algorithms, less hops

points: Batching trade-off — Writing 1,000 rows to a database one at a time: low throughput, low latency per write. Batching them into one INSERT: high throughput, but each individual row "waits" for the batch — higher latency.

p50 vs p99 latency — Median latency (p50) tells you the typical experience. p99 tells you the worst 1% of users. Interviewers love asking about p99 because that is where real problems hide (GC pauses, slow disk, cold cache).
Interview signal — When asked "how fast is your system?", always specify BOTH: "Average latency is 20ms at p50, 200ms at p99, with throughput of 50K QPS." Giving just one number is incomplete.
questions.followup:
"Your p99 latency is 10x your p50. What could cause that?"

Common culprits: garbage collection pauses (Java/Go), cache misses (the 1% that hit the DB instead of Redis), tail-of-fan-out (one slow downstream service out of many), cold database pages, or lock contention under high concurrency. Start investigating by looking at which requests are slow and what they have in common.

questions.question: What is a load balancer and why is it the first thing you draw after more than one server?
questions.tldr: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple servers so no single server is overwhelmed and failures are handled automatically.
questions.answer: The moment you say "horizontal scaling" in an interview, you need a load balancer. It sits between clients and your servers and decides who handles each request.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  C[Clients] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> S1[Server 1]
  LB --> S2[Server 2]
  LB --> S3[Server 3]
questions.diagramCaption: Load balancer distributes traffic across healthy servers

rows: Send to server with fewest active connections
Hash client IP to pick a server (sticky)
When same client must hit same server (stateful)

points: L4 vs L7 — Layer 4 (TCP/UDP level) is fast but dumb — it routes by IP and port. Layer 7 (HTTP level) is smarter — it can route by URL path, headers, or cookies. Most application load balancers are L7.
Health checks — The LB pings each server every few seconds. If a server stops responding, the LB stops sending traffic to it. This is how you get automatic failover.
Real-world examples — AWS ALB/NLB, Nginx, HAProxy, Google Cloud Load Balancer. In interviews, just say "load balancer" — you do not need to name a product unless asked.
The trade-off — A load balancer itself can become a single point of failure. Solution: run LBs in pairs (active-passive or active-active) with a floating IP.

questions.question: You mentioned a load balancer. Now I see "API gateway," "proxy," and "reverse proxy" in your design. What is the difference between all of these?
questions.tldr: Forward proxy = client-side intermediary. Reverse proxy = server-side intermediary. API gateway = reverse proxy with extra features (auth, rate limiting, routing). Load balancer = distributes traffic across servers.
questions.answer: These terms overlap and confuse everyone. The key is understanding where they sit and what they do. A system can use all four at the same time.
questions.diagram:
graph LR C[Client] --> FP["Forward Proxy
(client-side)"] FP --> Internet((Internet)) Internet --> RP["Reverse Proxy
(server-side)"] RP --> S[Servers]
questions.diagramCaption: Forward proxy sits in front of clients; reverse proxy sits in front of servers

rows: Hides client identity, filters outbound traffic, caches responses
Hides server identity, SSL termination, caching, compression
Distributes traffic across multiple servers using algorithms
Reverse proxy + auth + rate limiting + routing + request transformation

points: A reverse proxy and a load balancer are NOT the same — A reverse proxy can serve a single server (SSL termination, caching). A load balancer specifically distributes across multiple servers. In practice, tools like Nginx do both.

An API gateway IS a reverse proxy — But with added responsibilities: authentication, rate limiting, request/response transformation, API versioning, circuit breaking. If your system has microservices, you almost always need an API gateway.

When to use what — Single backend server? Reverse proxy (Nginx) for SSL + caching. Multiple identical servers? Load balancer. Multiple microservices with different APIs? API gateway to route /users to user-service and /orders to order-service.

Interview pattern — Draw: Client -> API Gateway -> Load Balancer -> App Servers. The gateway handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting). The LB handles traffic distribution. Separating them keeps each layer focused.
questions.followup:
"Can one tool do everything — reverse proxy, load balancing, AND API gateway?"

Yes. Nginx can be a reverse proxy, load balancer, and basic API gateway. Kong and Envoy are full API gateways with load balancing built in. But at scale, companies separate them: a dedicated API gateway for routing and auth, with a separate LB fleet for traffic distribution. The trade-off is simplicity (one tool) vs flexibility and scalability (separate concerns).

questions.question: What exactly is a cache and when does it make sense to add one? When does it NOT?
questions.tldr: A cache stores frequently accessed data in fast storage (RAM) to avoid hitting slow storage (disk/DB) repeatedly. Add when reads >> writes. Skip when data changes constantly.
questions.answer: Caching is the most common optimization you will propose in interviews. But it is not free — you are trading consistency for speed, and you are adding a layer that can fail or go stale.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  App[App Server] -- "1. Check cache" --> Cache[(Redis)]
  Cache -- "HIT: return fast" --> App
  App -- "2. MISS: query DB" --> DB[(Database)]
  DB -- "3. Store in cache" --> Cache
questions.diagramCaption: Cache-aside pattern: check cache first, fall back to DB on miss

rows: Write-heavy with data changing every second
Data must be real-time consistent (bank balances)
DB queries are expensive or slow
Cache size would exceed available RAM
Every request needs unique data (search results)

points: Cache hit rate — The percentage of requests served from cache. 90%+ is the goal. Below 50%, your cache is wasting RAM and adding latency (double lookup: cache miss + DB query).

Eviction policies — LRU (Least Recently Used): evict what has not been used lately. LFU (Least Frequently Used): evict what is used least often. TTL (Time To Live): evict after a set time. LRU is the default in most interviews.

Cache invalidation — "The two hardest problems in CS: cache invalidation and naming things." When the underlying data changes, the cache must be updated or cleared. Strategies: TTL-based expiry, write-through (update cache on every write), or event-driven invalidation.
The trade-off — Speed vs freshness. A cached response is fast but might be stale. You must decide: is it OK if a user sees data that is 30 seconds old? For a social feed, yes. For a stock price, no.

questions.question: What is a CDN (Content Delivery Network)? When would you add one to your design?
questions.tldr: A CDN caches static content (images, JS, CSS, video) at edge servers close to users worldwide, reducing latency from 150ms to <10ms.
questions.answer: If your users are global and you serve static content, a CDN is almost always the answer. It moves data closer to users instead of making every request travel to your origin server.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  U1[User: Tokyo] --> E1[Edge: Tokyo]
  U2[User: London] --> E2[Edge: London]
  E1 -. "cache miss" .-> Origin[Origin Server]
  E2 -. "cache miss" .-> Origin
questions.diagramCaption: CDN edge servers serve cached content; only cache misses hit origin
points: What it caches — Static files: images, videos, CSS, JavaScript, HTML pages. Some CDNs also cache API responses (dynamic content caching).
Push vs Pull CDN — Push: you upload content to CDN proactively. Pull: CDN fetches from origin on first request, then caches it. Pull is more common and simpler.
When to add a CDN — Global users, media-heavy content, high read traffic. If 80% of your bandwidth is serving images, a CDN offloads that entirely.
When NOT to use a CDN — Highly dynamic or personalized content that changes per user (dashboards, real-time data). Private content that requires auth on every request.
Real-world — CloudFront (AWS), Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly. Netflix serves 95%+ of traffic from its own CDN (Open Connect).

questions.question: I hear "database replication" in almost every design. What is it and what problem does it solve?
questions.tldr: Replication = copying data to multiple database servers. It improves read throughput, availability, and geographic performance. The trade-off is consistency lag.
questions.answer: Replication is how you survive a database server dying and how you serve reads faster. Without it, your database is a single point of failure.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  App[App Server] -- "writes" --> M[(Leader DB)]
  M -- "replicates" --> R1[(Replica 1)]
  M -- "replicates" --> R2[(Replica 2)]
  App -- "reads" --> R1
  App -- "reads" --> R2
questions.diagramCaption: Leader-follower replication: writes go to leader, reads spread across replicas

rows: One leader handles writes, followers handle reads
Simple but leader is a bottleneck for writes
Better write throughput but conflict resolution is hard
Leader waits for follower to confirm write
Leader writes and moves on; follower catches up
Lower latency but risk of data loss if leader dies

points: Read scaling — If your system is 90% reads, add 2 read replicas and you have tripled your read capacity without touching the leader.
Availability — If the leader dies, promote a replica to leader. Downtime is seconds to minutes instead of hours of restoring from backup.

The consistency trade-off — With async replication, a user might write data and then read from a replica that has not caught up yet. They see stale data for a few milliseconds. This is "replication lag" — the core trade-off.

Interview tip — When you draw a database, always mention replication. Say "leader with 2 async read replicas for availability and read scaling." It shows you think about failure and performance by default.

questions.question: What does "availability" mean in numbers, and how is it different from "reliability" and "fault tolerance"?
questions.tldr: Availability = percentage of time the system is up. Reliability = does it give correct results. Fault tolerance = can it keep running when parts fail.
questions.answer: These terms overlap but are not the same. Interviewers will probe whether you understand the differences and how each shapes your design.

rows: System is reachable and responds to requests
System produces correct results over time
A calculator that gives wrong answers is available but not reliable
System continues operating when components fail
One DB replica dies but reads keep working from the other
Data is not lost even after failure

questions.diagram:
graph TD FT["Fault Tolerance
Survive failures"] --> AV["Availability
Stay reachable"] AV --> REL["Reliability
Correct results"]
questions.diagramCaption: Fault tolerance enables availability; availability enables reliability
points: The nines table — 99% = 3.65 days/year down. 99.9% = 8.7 hours. 99.99% = 52 minutes. 99.999% = 5.2 minutes. Each additional nine is 10x harder and more expensive.
You achieve availability through redundancy — Multiple servers, multiple data centers, multiple regions. No single point of failure.
Fault tolerance is the mechanism — Health checks detect failures. Automatic failover switches traffic. Retries handle transient errors. Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures.
Interview pattern — "What is your SLA target?" → pick the nines → that determines how much redundancy you need. 3 nines: one region with failover. 4 nines: multi-region active-active.

questions.question: What are "consistency" and "eventual consistency"? Give me a concrete example of when each is OK.
questions.tldr: Strong consistency = every read returns the latest write. Eventual consistency = reads might be stale briefly, but will catch up. Most systems use eventual for speed.
questions.answer: This is the single most important trade-off in distributed systems. The CAP theorem says you cannot have strong consistency AND high availability during network partitions — you must choose.
questions.diagram:
graph LR W["Write: balance = $100"] --> L[(Leader)] L -- "sync" --> R1["Replica 1
$100"] L -- "async" --> R2["Replica 2
$90 (stale)"]
questions.diagramCaption: Sync replica is consistent; async replica may be briefly stale

rows: Every read sees the latest write
Reads may be stale; will converge eventually
Higher — keeps serving even if some replicas lag
Bank transfers, inventory counts, booking seats
Social feeds, like counts, analytics dashboards
When stale reads cause real harm (double spending)
When users lose money or data due to stale reads

points: Bank transfer example (strong) — You transfer $100 from Account A to Account B. If another user reads Account A before the transfer is replicated, they see the old balance and the $100 exists in both accounts. That is unacceptable.
Like count example (eventual) — A tweet shows 4,999 likes for 2 seconds before updating to 5,000. Nobody cares. Eventual consistency is perfectly fine here.

Interview signal — Never say "I want strong consistency everywhere." That is a red flag. Always ask: "What is the cost of a stale read?" If the cost is low, use eventual. If the cost is real money or data loss, use strong.

The trade-off — Strong consistency requires synchronous replication and coordination, which means higher latency and lower throughput. Eventual consistency is faster but requires your application to handle stale reads gracefully.
questions.followup:
"Can you have strong consistency for writes and eventual consistency for reads in the same system?"

Yes — this is extremely common. Writes go to a leader with synchronous replication for durability. Reads fan out to async replicas for speed. If a user needs to read-their-own-write, route that specific read to the leader. This hybrid is the default architecture for most production databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL with read replicas).

takeaways: Stateless > Stateful — Keep servers stateless; push state to a shared store (DB, Redis). This lets you scale horizontally and survive server failures.
Sync for user-facing, Async for background — Users need instant responses (sync). Background work like emails and analytics can wait (async queue).
Vertical first, Horizontal when needed — Start simple. Scale up the machine. When you hit limits, scale out with load balancers and stateless servers.
Latency vs Throughput — Latency = speed of one request. Throughput = capacity. Report both. Know your p50 and p99.
Cache when reads >> writes — Cache-aside with LRU eviction. Target 90%+ hit rate. Accept stale data as the trade-off.
Replicate your database — Leader-follower with async replicas is the default. It gives you read scaling and failover.
Availability nines — 99.9% = 8.7h/year. 99.99% = 52 min/year. More nines = more redundancy = more cost.
Strong vs Eventual consistency — Ask: "what is the cost of a stale read?" Low cost = eventual. High cost = strong. Most systems use a mix.

chapters.title: Scale From Zero to Millions of Users
chapters.subtitle: This is the most common opening question in system design interviews. The interviewer wants to see if you can think incrementally — not jump to the final architecture, but evolve it step by step.

questions.question: You have a brand new app with zero users. Walk me through how you would scale it to support millions.
questions.tldr: Scale in stages: single server → separate tiers → load balancer → cache → CDN → stateless → replication → sharding → multi-DC.
questions.answer: Never design for millions on day one. Start with the simplest thing that works, then add complexity only when the current stage becomes a bottleneck. Here is the roadmap:
questions.diagram:
graph LR A[Single Server
Web + DB + Cache] -.->|"traffic grows"| B[Separate Tiers
Web + DB + Cache] B -.->|"need redundancy"| C[Load Balanced
Multi-Server] C -.->|"go global"| D[Multi-DC
geoDNS] style A fill:#1c1c1e,color:#f5f5f7 style B fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff style C fill:#059669,color:#fff style D fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: The four stages of scaling — each stage solves a specific bottleneck
questions.diagram2:
graph TD
  LB[Load Balancer] --> W1[Web 1]
  LB --> W2[Web N]
  W1 & W2 --> Cache[(Cache)]
  W1 & W2 --> Master[(Master DB)]
  Master --> R1[(Replica)]
  Master --> R2[(Replica)]
  W1 --> MQ[Queue]
  MQ --> Workers[Workers]
  style LB fill:#059669,color:#fff
  style Cache fill:#f59e0b,color:#000
  style MQ fill:#f97316,color:#fff
questions.diagram2Caption: Stage 3 detail — load balancer, replicas, queue, and workers
points: Stage 1 (0–1K users) — Single server running everything. Why: You have zero traffic. The bottleneck is shipping the product, not scaling it. Separating tiers adds deployment complexity you don't need yet. One box means one deploy, one SSH, one set of logs. Why not a LB or cache? Because with 10 concurrent users, your single server responds in 2ms — adding infrastructure would be over-engineering.

Stage 2 (1K–100K users) — Separate web and DB onto different machines. Add a cache (Redis). Put static assets on a CDN. Why: Your single server is now CPU-bound (handling requests) AND I/O-bound (running queries) at the same time — they compete for resources. Separating them lets each optimize independently. Redis is added because the DB is the first bottleneck — the same 50 rows get queried thousands of times. A CDN is added because 60-80% of bandwidth is serving static files (images, JS, CSS) that never change. Why not a load balancer yet? Because one web server at this scale handles the traffic fine — you don't need redundancy until it maxes out.

Stage 3 (100K–1M users) — Multiple web servers behind a load balancer. Database read replicas. Message queue for async work. Stateless web tier. Why: One web server can't handle the request volume anymore (CPU at 80%+). A load balancer distributes traffic across multiple servers — but this only works if servers are stateless (no sessions in memory), so you move sessions to Redis. Read replicas are added because the DB is now the bottleneck again: 90% of queries are reads, so spreading reads across replicas cuts DB load by 2-3x. A message queue is added because slow tasks (emails, image resize) are blocking request threads — move them to background workers so users get instant responses. Why not sharding yet? Because read replicas + cache handle the read load, and write volume at this scale is still manageable on one leader.

Stage 4 (1M+ users) — Database sharding. Multiple data centers with geoDNS. Dedicated cache cluster. Possibly microservices decomposition. Why: The single database leader can't handle write volume anymore — it's the last remaining bottleneck. Sharding splits data across multiple databases so writes distribute evenly. Multi-DC is added because users are now global — a user in Tokyo hitting a US server gets 200ms latency on every request. GeoDNS routes them to the nearest DC for <20ms. The cache is now a dedicated cluster (not a single Redis instance) because cache size exceeds one machine's RAM and cache failures would cascade. Microservices are considered because the codebase is too large for one team to deploy safely — independent services let teams ship independently.
questions.followup:
"What triggers the move from one stage to the next?"

Metrics. When response times exceed your SLA (e.g., p99 > 500ms), when CPU or memory on any single machine consistently exceeds 70%, or when the database can't keep up with read/write load — those are your signals to move to the next stage. Never scale preemptively without data.

questions.question: Why do we separate the web tier from the data tier? What does that actually buy us?
questions.tldr: Independent scaling, failure isolation, and team autonomy.
questions.answer: When everything runs on one server, you have a single point of failure and you can't scale one layer without scaling all of them. Separating tiers means each layer can be optimized for its specific workload.
questions.diagram:
graph LR U[Users] --> W[Web Tier
CPU-bound
Scales horizontally] W --> D[(Data Tier
I/O-bound
Scales via replication + sharding)] style W fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff style D fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Two tiers, two different scaling strategies
points: Independent scaling — Web servers are CPU/memory bound. Databases are I/O bound. They need different scaling strategies.
Failure isolation — A web server crash doesn't corrupt your data. A slow query doesn't block your HTTP responses (with proper timeouts).
Team autonomy — The backend team and the DBA team can deploy, optimize, and upgrade independently.
Technology flexibility — You can switch from MySQL to PostgreSQL without touching the web tier, or from Node to Go without touching the database.

questions.question: Explain how a load balancer works. Where does it sit, and what algorithms can it use?
questions.tldr: It sits between users and servers, distributing traffic so no single server is overwhelmed.
questions.answer: Users connect to the load balancer's public IP. The LB forwards requests to backend servers on a private network. If a server goes down, the LB stops routing to it. When you add new servers, the LB automatically includes them.
questions.diagram:
graph TD U[Users
Internet] --> LB[Load Balancer
Public IP: 1.2.3.4] LB --> S1[Server 1
Private: 10.0.0.1] LB --> S2[Server 2
Private: 10.0.0.2] LB --> S3[Server 3
Private: 10.0.0.3] style LB fill:#059669,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Users see one IP — the LB decides which server actually handles the request

rows: More powerful servers get more requests
Route to server with fewest active connections
Hash client IP → always same server
Route by URL path, header, cookie

questions.followup:
"What's the difference between Layer 4 and Layer 7 load balancing?"

Layer 4 operates at TCP/UDP level — it sees only IP addresses and ports. Very fast, no packet inspection. Layer 7 operates at HTTP level — it can read URLs, headers, cookies, and make routing decisions based on content. Slower but much more flexible. Use L4 for raw throughput, L7 for smart routing.

questions.question: How does database replication work? What happens when the master goes down?
questions.tldr: Master handles writes, replicas handle reads. On master failure, promote a replica — but you may lose recent writes.
questions.answer: In master-slave replication, all writes go to one master database. The master streams changes to one or more read replicas. Since most workloads are 80-90% reads, this dramatically increases throughput.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  W[Web Servers] -->|"ALL writes"| M[(Master)]
  W -->|"reads"| R1[(Replica 1)]
  W -->|"reads"| R2[(Replica 2)]
  M -->|"replication stream"| R1
  M -->|"replication stream"| R2
  style M fill:#ff453a,color:#fff
  style R1 fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff
  style R2 fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Master handles writes, replicas handle reads — replication keeps them in sync
points: Master fails? — A replica is promoted to new master. Remaining replicas re-point. The old master, once recovered, rejoins as a replica.
Async replication — Fastest, but the promoted replica might be behind by a few seconds. Recent writes can be lost.
Semi-sync replication — Master waits for at least one replica to ACK before confirming the write. Slower, but no data loss on failover.
Sync replication — Master waits for ALL replicas to ACK. Safest, but latency is terrible. Rarely used across DCs.
questions.diagram2:
sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Master participant Replica Client->>Master: INSERT INTO users... Master->>Master: Write to WAL Master-->>Client: OK (async) Master->>Replica: Stream WAL changes Note over Replica: Applies changes
(may be seconds behind)
questions.diagram2Caption: Async replication — client gets OK before replica confirms
questions.followup:
"How do you handle replication lag where a user writes data and then immediately reads it back but hits a replica that hasn't caught up?"

Read-after-write consistency. Route the user's own reads to the master for a short window after a write (e.g., 5 seconds). Or use the replication timestamp — if the replica's position is behind the client's last write, route to master instead.

questions.question: When should you add a cache layer, and what caching strategy would you choose?
questions.tldr: Add cache when reads >> writes and the same data is requested repeatedly. Cache-aside is the most common strategy.
questions.answer: A cache sits between your application and the database. It stores frequently accessed data in memory (Redis, Memcached), reducing database load and cutting response times from milliseconds to microseconds.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App
  participant Redis as Cache (Redis)
  participant DB as Database
  App->>Redis: GET user:42
  alt HIT — data in cache
    Redis-->>App: Return data (0.1ms)
  else MISS — not in cache
    Redis-->>App: null
    App->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=42
    DB-->>App: Row data (5ms)
    App->>Redis: SET user:42 (TTL: 5min)
  end
questions.diagramCaption: Cache-aside pattern — the most common strategy in production

rows: App checks cache → miss → reads DB → writes to cache
Simple, but cache can go stale
<mark>Default choice.</mark> Read-heavy workloads where occasional staleness is acceptable (user profiles, product catalogs, feed timelines)
Cache itself fetches from DB on miss
Simpler app code, but cache lib must support it
When you want the same benefits as cache-aside but want the cache layer to own the fetch logic (content management systems, config stores)
Every write goes to cache AND DB simultaneously
Always consistent, but adds write latency
When you <mark>cannot tolerate stale reads</mark> — financial balances, inventory counts, session state
Write to cache only, async flush to DB
Fastest writes, but risk of data loss
Write-heavy workloads where <mark>speed matters more than durability</mark> — analytics counters, activity logs, view counts
Proactively refresh hot keys before TTL expires
Great for hot data, wasteful for cold data
Predictable hot keys that are read constantly — homepage data, leaderboards, trending content. Not worth it if access patterns are unpredictable

points: Cache stampede — When a popular key expires, hundreds of requests simultaneously hit the DB. Fix: use a lock so only one request fetches, others wait.
Cache invalidation — The hardest problem in CS. TTL-based expiry is simplest. Event-driven invalidation (on write, delete cache key) is more accurate.
What NOT to cache — Data that changes on every request, data with strong consistency requirements, or data accessed only once.

questions.question: How does a CDN work, and what are the gotchas?
questions.tldr: A CDN caches static content at edge servers worldwide, so users load assets from the nearest location instead of crossing oceans.
questions.answer: When a user in Tokyo requests an image, the CDN serves it from a Tokyo edge server (1ms) instead of your origin in Virginia (200ms). On the first request, the edge fetches from origin and caches it. Subsequent requests are served from cache.
questions.diagram:
graph TD U_Tokyo[User — Tokyo] -->|"1ms"| Edge_Tokyo[CDN Edge
Tokyo] U_London[User — London] -->|"1ms"| Edge_London[CDN Edge
London] Edge_Tokyo -.->|"cache miss: 200ms"| Origin[Origin Server
US-East] Edge_London -.->|"cache miss: 100ms"| Origin style Edge_Tokyo fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style Edge_London fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style Origin fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: CDN edge servers around the world — each caches content close to users
points: Push vs Pull CDN — Pull: content cached on first request (most common). Push: you upload content to CDN proactively (for large, predictable assets).
TTL is critical — Too short: frequent origin hits, defeating the purpose. Too long: users see stale content.
Cache busting — Use versioned URLs (app.v3.js or content hashes app.a1b2c3.js) to force instant updates without waiting for TTL.
Cost — CDN providers charge per GB transferred. Don't cache content that's rarely accessed — the cost of a cache miss + storage is higher than just serving from origin.
Dynamic content — Traditional CDNs only cache static assets. For dynamic: use edge computing (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge) to run logic at the edge.

questions.question: What does it mean to make the web tier stateless, and why is it critical for scaling?
questions.tldr: Move session data out of web servers into a shared store (Redis). Now any server can handle any request — enabling horizontal scaling and auto-scaling.
questions.answer:
In a stateful architecture, each server stores user session data in local memory. User A must always go to Server 1 (sticky sessions). If Server 1 dies, User A's session is gone. You can't freely add or remove servers.

In a stateless architecture, session data lives in an external store (Redis, DynamoDB). Every server is identical and interchangeable. The load balancer can send any request to any server.
questions.diagram:
graph LR U1[User A] -.->|"must go to"| S1["Server 1
holds session A"] U2[User B] -.->|"must go to"| S2["Server 2
holds session B"] style S1 fill:#ff453a,color:#fff style S2 fill:#ff453a,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Stateful — each user is locked to one server
questions.diagram2:
graph LR U[Any User] --> LB[Load Balancer] LB --> S1[Server 1] LB --> S2[Server N] S1 & S2 --> R[(Redis
Sessions)] style LB fill:#059669,color:#fff style R fill:#f59e0b,color:#000
questions.diagram2Caption: Stateless — any server can handle any request via shared session store
points: Auto-scaling — Stateless is a prerequisite. Auto-scaling groups add/remove servers based on load. If servers hold state, you can't kill them safely.
Zero-downtime deployments — Roll out new code by replacing servers one at a time. Stateless means no session data is lost during replacement.
The trade-off — Stateless adds a network hop to the session store on every request. Redis responds in <1ms, so the cost is negligible.
questions.followup:
"What about JWTs — aren't they stateless without needing Redis?"

JWTs move state to the client. Pro: no server-side session store needed. Con: you can't revoke them until they expire (unless you maintain a blacklist — which is... a server-side store). For most apps, server-side sessions in Redis are simpler and more secure.

questions.question: When would you introduce a message queue, and what problem does it solve?
questions.tldr: A queue decouples producers from consumers, absorbs traffic spikes, and enables async processing of slow tasks.
questions.answer: Any time your web server does something that doesn't need to happen in the request-response cycle — sending emails, processing images, generating reports, updating search indexes — put it on a queue. The web server responds immediately; workers process the task in the background.
questions.diagram:
graph LR W1[Web Server] -->|"send email task"| Q[Message Queue
Kafka / SQS] W2[Web Server] -->|"resize image task"| Q Q --> WK1[Email Worker] Q --> WK2[Image Worker] Q --> WK3[Image Worker] Q -.->|"failed tasks"| DLQ[Dead Letter Queue] style Q fill:#f97316,color:#fff style DLQ fill:#ff453a,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Producers fire tasks into the queue; workers consume at their own pace
points: Decoupling — Producers and consumers don't know about each other. You can change, scale, or replace either side independently.
Spike absorption — Black Friday sends 10x traffic? The queue buffers it. Workers chew through the backlog at a steady pace.
Independent scaling — Queue depth growing? Add more workers. No need to scale the entire web tier.
Retry and reliability — If a worker crashes mid-task, the message becomes visible again. After N failures, move it to a Dead Letter Queue for inspection.
Ordering — Kafka guarantees ordering within a partition. SQS FIFO guarantees ordering within a group. Standard SQS does not guarantee ordering.

questions.question: How do you decide between a SQL and NoSQL database?
questions.tldr: Use SQL when you need relationships, transactions, and complex queries. Use NoSQL when you need massive scale, flexible schema, or ultra-low latency.
questions.answer: This is not an either/or. Most production systems use both (polyglot persistence). The question is: for this specific data, which is the right tool?

rows: Activity feeds, sessions, IoT, real-time analytics

points: Choose SQL when — Your data has clear relationships, you need multi-row transactions, you need complex ad-hoc queries, or your team knows SQL well.
Choose NoSQL when — You need to scale writes horizontally, your schema changes frequently, you need sub-millisecond reads, or your access pattern is simple (key → value).
Common combo — PostgreSQL for user accounts and orders (needs ACID) + Redis for sessions and cache + Kafka for event streaming + Elasticsearch for search.

Interview signal — Never say "I'd use NoSQL because it's faster." Say: "For this specific data and access pattern, NoSQL fits because [reason]. But the user's billing data needs ACID, so that stays in Postgres." Showing you pick per use case is what separates senior answers.
questions.diagram2:
graph LR subgraph Facebook FB1[PostgreSQL
User accounts, ads billing] FB2[Cassandra
Inbox, activity feed] FB3[Memcached/TAO
Social graph cache] end subgraph Instagram IG1[PostgreSQL
User profiles, follows] IG2[Cassandra
Feed, stories, DMs] IG3[Redis
Session, rate limiting] end subgraph Netflix NF1[MySQL
Billing, subscriptions] NF2[Cassandra
Viewing history, 1T+ rows] NF3[Elasticsearch
Search catalog] end style FB1 fill:#30d158,color:#000 style FB2 fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style FB3 fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000 style IG1 fill:#30d158,color:#000 style IG2 fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style IG3 fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000 style NF1 fill:#30d158,color:#000 style NF2 fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style NF3 fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000
questions.diagram2Caption: Real-world polyglot persistence — green = SQL, purple = NoSQL, orange = cache/search
questions.followup:
"Give me concrete examples — when would a real company pick one over the other?"

CompanyUse CaseDB ChoiceWhy This DB
FacebookUser accounts, ad billingPostgreSQL (SQL)Money is involved. Ad billing requires ACID transactions — if an advertiser is charged $10K, that row must be consistent, not "eventually" correct. Relational joins link advertisers → campaigns → impressions.
FacebookInbox, activity feedCassandra (NoSQL)2B+ users generate billions of feed items/day. Write throughput is the bottleneck, not complex queries. Access pattern is simple: "give me the last 50 items for user X." Cassandra handles this at 1M+ writes/sec across hundreds of nodes.
InstagramUser profiles, follow graphPostgreSQL (SQL)Profiles have clear relationships (follows, blocks, likes). "Who follows who" requires joins. Schema is stable — a user profile hasn't changed shape in years.
InstagramStories, DMs, feedCassandra (NoSQL)500M daily stories, each expires in 24h. Massive write volume with a simple access pattern (latest stories for user X). Data is ephemeral — TTL-based expiry in Cassandra handles automatic deletion.
NetflixBilling, subscriptionsMySQL (SQL)Subscription = financial data. "Charge user $15.99/month" must be ACID. Regulatory compliance requires audit trails with relational integrity.
NetflixViewing historyCassandra (NoSQL)230M+ subscribers, each with hundreds of watch events. Over 1 trillion rows. Access pattern: "what did user X watch recently?" — a single partition read. No joins needed. Cassandra scales linearly by adding nodes.
NetflixContent searchElasticsearchFull-text search with fuzzy matching, filters (genre, language, rating), and ranking. SQL LIKE queries can't do this at scale. Elasticsearch is purpose-built for search with inverted indexes.
UberTrip data, driver locationsGoogle Spanner / Schemaless (NoSQL)Location data changes every 4 seconds for millions of drivers. Needs geo-indexing and extreme write throughput. Trip history is append-only — no complex joins needed.
ShopifyOrders, inventoryMySQL (SQL)E-commerce: "decrement inventory by 1 AND create order row" must be atomic. If the transaction fails halfway, you'd either oversell or lose an order. ACID is non-negotiable.
DiscordMessagesCassandra → ScyllaDB (NoSQL)Billions of messages/day. Access pattern: "last 50 messages in channel X." Originally Cassandra, migrated to ScyllaDB (C++ rewrite of Cassandra) for lower tail latency at p99.

The pattern: SQL for money, identity, and relationships. NoSQL for high-volume, simple-access, time-series or ephemeral data. Most companies use 3-5 different databases — each chosen for a specific access pattern.

questions.question: What is database sharding, how do you choose a shard key, and what are the biggest challenges?
questions.tldr: Sharding splits one database into many, each holding a subset of data. The shard key determines the split. Get it wrong and you get hotspots.
questions.answer: When a single database can't handle the write load even after vertical scaling, you split it horizontally. Each shard is an independent database that holds a portion of the data. A routing layer decides which shard handles each query.
questions.diagram:
graph TD App[Application] --> Router[Shard Router
hash of user_id mod N] Router -->|"0"| S0[(Shard 0
users 0-24M)] Router -->|"1"| S1[(Shard 1
users 25-49M)] Router -->|"2"| S2[(Shard 2
users 50-74M)] Router -->|"3"| S3[(Shard 3
users 75-100M)] style Router fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Hash-based sharding — user_id determines which shard stores the data
points: Shard key selection is everything — The key must distribute data evenly AND match your most common query pattern. user_id is good for social apps. order_date is bad (all writes go to the "today" shard).
Hotspot / celebrity problem — If Justin Bieber's data is all on Shard 2, that shard is 100x more loaded. Fix: further partition within the shard, or dedicate shards for hot users.

Cross-shard joins are gone — SELECT orders JOIN users across shards requires fetching from multiple databases and joining in the application. Solution: denormalize — store copies of data where it's needed.
Resharding is painful — Adding a shard means moving data. Consistent hashing (Chapter 5) minimizes how much data moves.
Operational overhead — Every backup, migration, schema change, and monitoring config now multiplies by the number of shards.
questions.followup:
"If sharding is so painful, what do you try first?"

In order: (1) Optimize queries and add indexes. (2) Vertical scaling (bigger machine). (3) Read replicas for read-heavy workloads. (4) Cache aggressively with Redis. (5) Archive old data. Only when ALL of these are exhausted do you shard. Sharding is the last resort, not the first impulse.

questions.question: How would you design a system that operates across multiple data centers?
questions.tldr: geoDNS routes users to the nearest DC. Cross-DC data replication provides availability. The hard part is data consistency.
questions.answer: For global users, you need multiple data centers. Each DC is a fully independent deployment — its own web servers, cache, database. Users are routed to the nearest DC via geoDNS. If one DC goes down, DNS shifts traffic to another.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  US["Users US"] -->|"geoDNS"| DC1["DC US-East"]
  EU["Users EU"] -->|"geoDNS"| DC2["DC EU-West"]
  DC1 <-->|"async replication"| DC2
  style DC1 fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff
  style DC2 fill:#059669,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: geoDNS routes users to the nearest data center
questions.diagram2:
graph LR DB1[(Primary US)] <-->|"bi-directional
replication"| DB2[(Primary EU)] style DB1 fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff style DB2 fill:#059669,color:#fff
questions.diagram2Caption: Each DC has a primary DB — they replicate to each other asynchronously
points: Traffic routing — geoDNS resolves the same domain to different IPs based on the user's geographic location.
Data replication — Async replication across DCs (accepting eventual consistency). Each DC can have a primary database for writes in its region.
Conflict resolution — If two users update the same record in different DCs simultaneously: last-write-wins (simplest), vector clocks (accurate), CRDTs (automatic merge).
Session handling — Use a global session store with cross-region replication, or stateless JWTs so no session store is needed.
Failover — If DC1 goes down, DNS TTL determines how fast users switch to DC2 (typically 30–60 seconds). For faster failover, use health-check-based routing (AWS Route 53).
questions.followup:
"How does this relate to the CAP theorem?"

Multi-DC is where CAP becomes real. During a network partition between DCs, you must choose: Consistency (reject writes until the other DC is reachable) or Availability (accept writes and resolve conflicts later). Most consumer apps choose availability (AP). Banking and inventory systems may choose consistency (CP).

questions.question: Draw me the final architecture of a system that serves millions of users globally.
questions.tldr: CDN for static assets, geoDNS for routing, LB → stateless web servers → cache → master + replicas, message queue → workers, all replicated across DCs.
questions.answer: This is the target architecture. Every component exists because a specific bottleneck demanded it. When you draw this in an interview, explain why each component is there — not just what it is.
questions.diagram:
graph LR Users[Users] -->|"static"| CDN[CDN] Users -->|"API"| LB[Load Balancer] CDN -.->|"miss"| S3[S3] LB --> Web[Web Tier
stateless] style CDN fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style LB fill:#059669,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Front tier — CDN serves static assets, LB routes API requests
questions.diagram2:
graph LR
  Web[Web Tier] --> Cache[(Redis Cache)]
  Web -->|"writes"| Master[(Master DB)]
  Cache -.->|"miss"| Master
  Master --> R1[(Replica)]
  Master --> R2[(Replica)]
  Web --> MQ[Queue]
  MQ --> Workers[Workers]
  style Cache fill:#f59e0b,color:#000
  style MQ fill:#f97316,color:#fff
  style Master fill:#ff453a,color:#fff
questions.diagram2Caption: Back tier — cache, database with replicas, and async workers
points: CDN → Solves latency for static assets globally
Load Balancer → Distributes traffic, eliminates single point of failure
Stateless Web Servers → Enables horizontal scaling and auto-scaling
Session Store (Redis) → Enables stateless web tier
Cache Layer (Redis) → Reduces database load by 80-90%
Master + Read Replicas → Handles read-heavy workloads; failover for availability
Message Queue → Decouples async work; absorbs spikes
Worker Pool → Processes background tasks independently
NoSQL + Search → Specialized stores for specialized access patterns

takeaways: Scale incrementally — Don't design for 10M users on day one. Each stage solves a specific bottleneck.
Separate tiers — Web, cache, database. Independent scaling, independent failures.
Stateless web tier — The prerequisite for horizontal scaling. Move state to Redis.
Cache is your biggest lever — A well-placed cache reduces DB load by 80-90%. Know the 5 strategies.
Replication before sharding — Read replicas handle most read-heavy workloads. Shard only as a last resort.
Queue everything async — If it doesn't need to happen in the request-response cycle, put it on a queue.
Every component is a trade-off — Never say "always use X." Name the trade-off. That's what separates senior from junior.

chapters.title: APIs and REST
chapters.subtitle: What makes a good API — REST principles, resource naming, versioning, pagination, idempotency, and the mistakes that fail interviews.

questions.question: I hand you a blank whiteboard and say "design the API for a ride-sharing app." Where do you start?
questions.tldr: Start with resources (nouns), not endpoints (verbs). Identify the core entities, then map HTTP methods to operations on them.
questions.answer: The biggest mistake candidates make is jumping straight to listing endpoints like /createRide, /getDriver. That's thinking in functions, not resources. REST is about resources — nouns, not verbs. The HTTP method IS the verb.
points: Nouns, not verbs in URLs — /rides not /createRide. The method (POST, GET, PUT, DELETE) is the verb. If your URL contains a verb, it's not RESTful.
Plural, always — /users not /user. Even for single items: GET /users/42.
Max 2 levels of nesting — /users/:id/rides is fine. /users/:id/rides/:rid/payments/:pid/refunds is not. Flatten with query params or top-level resources.
questions.followup:
"Good. Now how do you handle actions that don't map cleanly to CRUD — like cancelling a ride or rating a driver?"

Use sub-resources: POST /rides/:id/cancel, POST /rides/:id/rating. These are actions on a resource, modeled as a new sub-resource. Some teams prefer a status field update via PATCH: PATCH /rides/:id { status: "cancelled" }. Either works — pick one convention and be consistent.

questions.question: OK, you said POST to create a ride. The user's phone loses connection and the request gets sent twice. Now they have two rides. How do you prevent that?
questions.tldr: Use an idempotency key — a unique client-generated ID sent in the header so the server can deduplicate retries.
questions.answer: This is the idempotency problem. GET, PUT, and DELETE are naturally idempotent — calling them twice produces the same result. But POST is NOT idempotent — two identical POST requests create two resources. In a ride-sharing app, that means two charges to the user.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant S as Server
  participant DB as Database
  C->>S: POST /rides (Key: abc-123)
  S->>DB: Check: key abc-123 exists?
  DB-->>S: No
  S->>DB: Create ride + store key
  S-->>C: 201 Created (ride_42)
  Note over C: Network timeout, retry
  C->>S: POST /rides (Key: abc-123)
  S->>DB: Check: key abc-123 exists?
  DB-->>S: Yes, ride_42
  S-->>C: 200 OK (ride_42, same result)
questions.diagramCaption: Idempotency key pattern — Stripe uses this for every payment API call
points: Client generates the key — Usually a UUID. Sent as header: Idempotency-Key: 550e8400-e29b.... Server stores it with the response for 24-48 hours.

Stripe's approach — Every POST /charges requires an idempotency key. If the same key is sent again, Stripe returns the cached response instead of creating a duplicate charge. This is the gold standard — mention it in interviews.
Where to store keys — Redis with TTL (fast lookups, auto-expiry) or a database column with a unique constraint (simpler, but slower).
PUT is naturally idempotent — PUT /users/42 { name: "Alice" } always produces the same result regardless of how many times you call it. That's why updates prefer PUT over POST.
questions.followup:
"What if the server crashes between creating the ride and storing the idempotency key?"

Wrap both in a database transaction. Insert the ride row and the idempotency key row in the same transaction. If the server crashes mid-way, the transaction rolls back and neither is persisted — the client retries cleanly. Atomicity is the key.

questions.question: Your API returns a list of 10 million rides. How do you paginate it — and don't say "just use page numbers."
questions.tldr: Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets. Offset pagination breaks at scale — it gets slower with every page and can skip/duplicate items.
questions.answer: There are two approaches, and the choice matters at scale:

rows: Slow at high offsets (DB scans skipped rows). Items shift if data changes between requests.
No random page access. Can't jump to "page 500."

points: Why offset breaks — SELECT * FROM rides OFFSET 999980 LIMIT 20 forces the DB to scan and discard 999,980 rows. On page 50,000, this query takes seconds. Offset pagination is O(n) where n is the offset.
Cursor uses an indexed column — SELECT * FROM rides WHERE id > 'ride_499' ORDER BY id LIMIT 20. The DB uses the index to jump directly. Constant time regardless of position.
Real-world examples — Stripe, Slack, and GitHub all use cursor-based pagination. Twitter's timeline API uses max_id as a cursor. Only simple admin dashboards use offset.
Response format — Always include a next_cursor field: { data: [...], next_cursor: "ride_519", has_more: true }. The client passes it back as ?after=ride_519.

questions.question: You're building a public API that thousands of developers depend on. You need to change the response format. How do you not break everyone?
questions.tldr: Version your API. URL versioning (/v1/) is the clearest approach for public APIs. Never change an existing field's meaning without a new version.
questions.answer: API versioning is how you evolve without breaking existing clients. There are three main strategies — each with different trade-offs:

rows: Obvious, easy to test in browser

points: Stripe's date-based versioning — Stripe-Version: 2024-10-28. Each account is pinned to the API version from their first request. Breaking changes are isolated. This is the most developer-friendly versioning ever designed.

Breaking vs non-breaking changes — Adding a new field is non-breaking (backward compatible). Removing a field, renaming a field, or changing a type IS breaking. Real-world lesson: Stripe renamed amount to amount_cents — it broke hundreds of integrations.
Interview signal — Say: "For a public API, I'd use URL versioning because it's explicit and easy to deprecate. For internal microservices, I'd use header versioning to keep URLs clean."
